Repairs remain incomplete 45 days after the due date; Servicer must have already worked with Borrower to determine and document an extension per the section.
business_rules.extraction_notes: Explicit 30-day threshold with 'must' actions; downstream documentation specifics appear in later subsections.

examples: Seller/Servicer acting as Servicer for Freddie Mac-owned loans
Subservicer performing collections and reporting under oversight
Servicer implementing a remediation plan after reporting errors

business_rules.rule_name: Servicer record destruction barred before 3 or 6 years
business_rules.description: The Servicer must not surrender or destroy canceled checks, bank statements, or other Mortgage records/accounts without Freddie Mac’s prior written approval. If permitted by applicable law, the Servicer may destroy original records (including mortgage ledger cards) three years after the Mortgage is fully paid, or if accelerated, six years after it is fully paid.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to any Mortgage serviced for Freddie Mac, incl. paid off/foreclosed
business_rules.consequences: If violated, records may be deemed improperly retained; audit issues arise
business_rules.exceptions: Allowed by law: destroy originals 3 yrs after payoff; 6 yrs if accelerated

source_reference.source_text: 50.4 Release or destruction of records (12/07/05) Without Freddie Mac's prior written approval, the Servicer must not surrender or destroy any canceled checks, bank statements or other records and accounts for any Mortgage serviced for Freddie Mac, including any Mortgage paid in full, sold, foreclosed or otherwise liquidated. However, when permitted by applicable law, the Servicer may destroy any original records, including mortgage ledger cards : • Three years after the date a Mortgage is fully paid, or • If the Mortgage is accelerated, six years after the date it is fully paid

examples: Mortgage paid in full on 2023-05-01: originals may be destroyed on/after 2026-05-01 if permitted by law.
Mortgage accelerated then paid in full on 2021-09-15: originals may be destroyed on/after 2027-09-15 if permitted by law.
Servicer wants to shred canceled checks 12 months after payoff: prohibited unless Freddie Mac gives prior written approval.
Seller/Servicer acting as Servicer for Freddie Mac-owned loans
Subservicer performing collections and reporting under oversight
Servicer implementing a remediation plan after reporting errors

business_rules.rule_name: Remit 7 collection types via myOptigo Servicer Remittance
business_rules.description: The Servicer must remit specified Mortgage collections and fees to Freddie Mac via Freddie Mac’s myOptigo Servicer Remittance system, including monthly principal and interest, curtailments, paid-in-full proceeds, Tax Exempt Bond Credit Enhancement fees, default interest, late fees, and interest reimbursement/noncompliance fees assessed by Freddie Mac. Proceeds of miscellaneous transactions not in that list must be remitted by wire transfer to Multifamily Cash Management.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to remittances for Mortgages serviced for Freddie Mac under Ch. 50
business_rules.consequences: Correct channel used supports remittance compliance; wrong channel risks fees/delay
business_rules.exceptions: REO sales/misc. remitted per Section 53.9 (not via this rule)

source_reference.source_text: 50.7 Summary of remittance requirements and transfer method ( 10/19 /23) The timing for remittance differs depending upon whether the Mortgage is a Gold PC® Securitized Mortgage, 55-Day Multi PC Securitized Mortgage, Tax Exempt Bond Credit Enhancement Mortgage or other Mortgage. The Servicer must remit the following Mortgage collections and fees to Freddie Mac via Freddie Mac’s myOptigo ® Servicer Remittance system (see Section 53.7): 1. Monthly principal and interest collections 2. Curtailments, including any applicable prepayment premiums 3. Proceeds from Mortgages paid in full including any applicable prepayment premium 4. Tax Exempt Bond Credit Enhancement fees 5. Default interest 6. Late fees 7. Interest reimbursement fees and noncompliance fees assessed by Freddie Mac The Servicer must remit proceeds of miscellaneous transactions not included above by wire transfer to Multifamily Cash Management .

examples: Monthly P&I collected on 10/01: remitted through myOptigo Servicer Remittance, not wire.
Borrower makes a curtailment with a prepayment premium: both amounts remitted via myOptigo.
Miscellaneous transaction not listed (e.g., other non-specified proceeds): remitted by wire to Multifamily Cash Management.
Seller/Servicer acting as Servicer for Freddie Mac-owned loans
Subservicer performing collections and reporting under oversight
Servicer implementing a remediation plan after reporting errors

business_rules.rule_name: Remittance compliance measured by effective date of receipt
business_rules.description: Freddie Mac uses the effective date of receipt to determine whether the Servicer’s remittance was made in compliance with interim and monthly remittance requirements. Failure to comply with remittance and transfer requirements may result in an interest reimbursement fee per Section 53.11.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when evaluating interim/monthly remittance compliance under Sections 53.9/53.10
business_rules.consequences: Compliant receipt timing avoids fees; noncompliance may trigger interest reimbursement fee
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.source_text: Freddie Mac will use effective date of receipt to determine if the remittance has been made in compliance with the interim and monthly remittance requirements as set forth in Sections 53.9 and 53.10, respectively. Failure of the Servicer to comply with the remittance and transfer requirements may result in an interest reimbursement fee as set forth in Section 53.11.

examples: Servicer initiates wire on due date but Freddie Mac receives next day: receipt date governs compliance determination.
myOptigo submission transmitted but rejected and re-submitted later: effective date of receipt of usable remittance controls.
Servicer remits late relative to Section 53.10 schedule: may incur an interest reimbursement fee under Section 53.11.
Seller/Servicer acting as Servicer for Freddie Mac-owned loans
Subservicer performing collections and reporting under oversight
Servicer implementing a remediation plan after reporting errors

dependencies.rationale: 039_003 states Freddie Mac uses the effective date of receipt to determine whether remittance was made in compliance with interim and monthly remittance requirements and may assess an interest reimbursement fee for failure. 027_006 defines concrete monthly remittance timing; 039_003 is the enforcement/assessment layer that validates 027_006 compliance based on receipt timing.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If 027_006 is not met, 039_003 indicates the remittance will be deemed noncompliant and may trigger an interest reimbursement fee and escalation.

dependencies.rationale: 031_007 requires REO proceeds be remitted by wire transfer immediately after settlement, with crediting rules for after-4 p.m. wires. 039_003 states Freddie Mac uses effective date of receipt to determine compliance with remittance requirements and may assess an interest reimbursement fee for failures. The effective-date concept is used to validate compliance with 031_007 timing/crediting expectations.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If 031_007 timing is missed, Freddie Mac can deem remittance noncompliant based on receipt crediting rules, potentially leading to fees or escalation pathways referenced in 039_003.

dependencies.rationale: 031_008 sets a 10 Business Day deadline after Freddie Mac’s letter for remitting REO repurchase proceeds. 039_003 provides Freddie Mac’s mechanism for determining remittance compliance based on effective receipt date and the consequence of an interest reimbursement fee. The receipt-date rule is used to validate whether the 10-day remittance was timely received.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If 031_008 is violated, Freddie Mac may treat remittance as noncompliant and assess fees/escalate under the framework described in 039_003.

business_rules.rule_name: Equity conflict statement due within 3 business days of request
business_rules.description: If an Equity Conflict of Interest (as defined in Section 2.25) exists, the Servicer must deliver to Multifamily Asset Management, Borrower Transactions a written statement disclosing the nature and extent of the conflict within three Business Days after receiving the Borrower’s request for approval of the Transfer of Ownership.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to SBL Transfers of Ownership when an Equity Conflict of Interest exists
business_rules.consequences: Timely disclosure supports review; late/missing disclosure risks processing delays/noncompliance
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.source_text: c. Required Servicer disclosure ( 08/26/21) If an Equity Conflict of Interest exists as defined in Section 2.25, then the Servicer must provide to Multifamily Asset Management, Borrower Transactions a written statement that discloses the nature and extent of that Equity Conflict of Interest within three Business Days after receiving the Borrower’s request for approval of the Transfer of Ownership.

examples: Borrower requests approval on Monday and an equity conflict exists: Servicer must provide written disclosure by Thursday (if all are Business Days).
Servicer identifies equity conflict after receipt: the 3-Business-Day clock still runs from receiving the request.
No equity conflict exists: this specific 3-Business-Day written disclosure rule does not apply.
Seller/Servicer acting as Servicer for Freddie Mac-owned loans
Subservicer performing collections and reporting under oversight
Servicer implementing a remediation plan after reporting errors

business_rules.rule_name: Workout requests must be referred per 12/14/23
business_rules.description: The Servicer must refer all Borrower written requests for any type of workout (including Mortgage modifications or repayment plans) to Multifamily Asset Resolution. Freddie Mac will negotiate the workout plan, prepare documents, and notify the Servicer of any Mortgage changes.
business_rules.conditions: Borrower submits a written request for a workout (modification or repayment plan).
business_rules.consequences: If complied, Freddie Mac negotiates and documents; if violated, escalates to Freddie Mac and may trigger servicing noncompliance findings.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in the cited section.

source_reference.source_text: 44.6 Enforcement actions – workouts (12/14/23)  The Servicer must refer all Borrower written requests for any type of workout, including Mortgage  modifications or repayment plans, to Multifamily Asset Resolution .  Freddie Mac will negotiate the workout plan, have all necessary documents prepared, and notify the Servicer of any changes to the Mortgage.

examples: Borrower emails a signed hardship letter requesting a repayment plan; servicer forwards it to Multifamily Asset Resolution rather than drafting terms internally.
Borrower delivers a written modification request at month-end; servicer logs receipt date and refers it to Multifamily Asset Resolution the same day for Freddie Mac negotiation.
Servicer mistakenly offers borrower a proprietary repayment plan without referral; Freddie Mac rejects it and issues a servicing finding requiring corrective action.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Servicing noncompliance finding; corrective action plan

examples: Seller/Servicer acting as Servicer for Freddie Mac-owned loans
Subservicer performing collections and reporting under oversight
Servicer implementing a remediation plan after reporting errors

business_rules.rule_name: Reimbursable costs need approval per 12/14/23
business_rules.description: Before incurring any expense that is reimbursable under enforcement-action servicing, the Servicer must request in writing and receive approval from Freddie Mac. The approval request must include a copy of Reserve and suspense account balances and describe the source and reason for any suspense account funds.
business_rules.conditions: Servicer plans to incur an expense intended to be reimbursable under enforcement action servicing.
business_rules.consequences: If met, reimbursable expenses may be billed/paid; if violated, reimbursement may be denied and expense remains Servicer’s obligation.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in the cited excerpt.

source_reference.source_text: c. Reimbursement of costs advanced by Servicer  (12/14/23 ) The Servicer must request in writing and receive approval from Freddie Mac before incurring any expense reimbursable under this section.  The Servicer must attach to its approval request a copy of the Reserve and suspense account balances with a description of the source and reason for any suspense account funds.

examples: Servicer wants to order an environmental assessment during foreclosure; it sends a written approval request with reserve and suspense balance screenshots before ordering.
Servicer incurs property condition report costs without prior written approval; Freddie Mac denies reimbursement and the servicer absorbs the cost.
Servicer submits an approval request but omits suspense account source details; Freddie Mac requests resubmission before approving.
Seller/Servicer acting as Servicer for Freddie Mac-owned loans
Subservicer performing collections and reporting under oversight
Servicer implementing a remediation plan after reporting errors

business_rules.rule_name: Replacement reserve forms due within 10 days
business_rules.description: Within 10 days following funding of a Replacement Reserve, the Servicer must deliver to Freddie Mac an original executed custodial account letter agreement form. If the custodial account is held by an institution other than the Servicer, use Form 1058; if held by the Servicer, use Form 1060.
business_rules.conditions: Replacement Reserve becomes funded and a Replacement Reserve Custodial Account is maintained by an institution.
business_rules.consequences: If met, Freddie Mac receives required custodial account documentation; if violated, file is deficient and Freddie Mac may require remediation or impose servicing findings.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in the cited excerpt.

source_reference.section_id: Guide Chapter 39.6(c) (02/24/26) (forms requirement text on Page 22)
source_reference.source_text: Within 10 days following funding of the Replacement Reserve, the Servicer must deliver to Freddie Mac an original of one of the following forms, as applicable, executed on behalf of the institution that maintains the Replacement Reserve Custodial Account:  • If the Custodial Account for the Replacement Reserve is held by an institution other than the Servicer, Form 1058, Letter Agreement for Servicer’s Reserve Custodial Account  • If the Custodial Account for the Replacement Reserve is held by the Servicer, Form 1060, Letter Agreement for Reserve Custodial Account

examples: Borrower begins funding reserves on June 1; servicer sends the original executed Form 1058 to Freddie Mac by June 11.
Servicer holds the custodial account internally; it obtains execution of Form 1060 and delivers the original within 10 days after the first deposit posts.
Servicer delivers a scanned copy only and misses the 10-day deadline; Freddie Mac requests the original and cites a documentation deficiency.
Seller/Servicer acting as Servicer for Freddie Mac-owned loans
Subservicer performing collections and reporting under oversight
Servicer implementing a remediation plan after reporting errors

dependencies.rationale: 040_006 requires delivering an executed custodial account letter agreement within 10 days following funding of a Replacement Reserve. 033_004 requires establishing and maintaining reserve custodial accounts with specific separation and titling. The letter agreement requirement is tied to the custodial account arrangement; the account setup is a logical prerequisite to producing the correct executed letter agreement.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If 033_004 is noncompliant (wrong account structure/titling), the letter agreement under 040_006 may be incorrect or rejected, causing file deficiencies and potential remediation.

dependencies.rationale: 040_006 requires delivery of a custodial account letter agreement (Form 1058/1060) for a funded Replacement Reserve custodial account. 029_003 defines eligibility criteria for FDIC-insured depositories used for custodial accounts. The custodial account documentation presumes an eligible depository selection and ongoing eligibility monitoring.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If 029_003 fails (ineligible bank), 040_006 documentation may need to be redone after moving the account, causing compliance deficiencies and operational delays.

business_rules.rule_name: Critical reserve disbursement needs risk rating 6
business_rules.description: The Servicer may only authorize a Replacement Reserve disbursement for a “critical replacement” when all listed conditions are met, including: (i) the Property secures a Mortgage with a Risk Rating of six or less and the Mortgage is not in default; (ii) the disbursement is for upgrades that add value and prevent an adverse condition; (iii) a supervisor or higher-level manager approves; and (iv) no credit enhancement is provided by any non-party to the Mortgage. If conditions are not met, Freddie Mac approval must be sought.
business_rules.conditions: Servicer is considering authorizing a Replacement Reserve disbursement for a critical replacement item.
business_rules.consequences: If met, servicer may authorize; if violated, Freddie Mac approval is required and unauthorized disbursement may be a servicing breach.
business_rules.exceptions: If any condition not met, must seek Freddie Mac approval.

source_reference.source_text: The Servicer may only authorize a disbursement for a critical replacement described in Section 39.6(b)(2) when all of the following additional conditions are met:  i. The Property secures a Mortgage that has a Risk Rating of six or less and is not in default.  ii. The disbursement is for capital replacement items that are upgrades which add value to the continued operation of the Property (“critical replacement”), if each of the following conditions is satisfied: iii. A supervisor or higher -level manager on the Servicer’s staff has approved the disbursement for the additional capital replacement items.  iv. No credit enhancement has been provided by any person or entity that is not a party to the Mortgage.

examples: Loan risk rating is 5 and current; servicer supervisor approves reserve use to replace failing boiler with higher-efficiency unit that prevents imminent outages—authorized without Freddie Mac pre-approval.
Loan risk rating is 7 (above 6); servicer must submit a General Servicing Request to obtain Freddie Mac approval before disbursing reserve funds.
A third-party guarantor provides a credit enhancement not a party to the mortgage; condition (iv) fails and Freddie Mac approval is required before disbursement.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Servicing breach; Freddie Mac approval required; remediation

examples: Seller/Servicer acting as Servicer for Freddie Mac-owned loans
Subservicer performing collections and reporting under oversight
Servicer implementing a remediation plan after reporting errors

dependencies.rationale: 040_007 conditions authorization of a 'critical replacement' disbursement on the Mortgage not being in default. 002_002 defines when a Mortgage Default occurs and when the mortgage is classified as a Defaulted Mortgage. The disbursement-authorization logic depends on the correct default classification.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If 002_002 is misapplied, the Servicer may improperly authorize disbursements while the loan is in default (servicing breach) or improperly deny disbursements when the loan is not in default (unnecessary operational disruption).

dependencies.rationale: 040_006 requires delivering executed custodial account letter agreements shortly after funding of a Replacement Reserve. 040_007 governs when the Servicer may authorize a Replacement Reserve disbursement for a critical replacement. In practice, disbursement controls and approvals are typically executed after custodial account setup/documentation is completed, to ensure controlled custody and audit trail.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If 040_006 is missed, disbursements may proceed with deficient custodial documentation, increasing audit/compliance findings and potential Freddie Mac-directed remediation.

business_rules.rule_name: Material vendor cannot access borrower PPI 10/20/22
business_rules.description: For accounting and reporting responsibilities, the Servicer may not use a Material Vendor to perform prohibited functions, including reviewing Borrower/Guarantor financial information or protected personal information, managing the Borrower relationship (including communications), communicating with Freddie Mac on behalf of the Servicer, or performing any function likely to give the Material Vendor access to any Borrower/Guarantor financial or personal information. Delegation of these prohibited functions is only permitted to a Servicing Agent upon Freddie Mac approval.
business_rules.conditions: Servicer is outsourcing accounting/reporting tasks to a Material Vendor or considering delegation to a Servicing Agent.
business_rules.consequences: If complied, outsourcing remains within permitted boundaries; if violated, Freddie Mac may cite noncompliance and require termination/remediation and impose audit findings.
business_rules.exceptions: May delegate to a Servicing Agent only with Freddie Mac approval.

source_reference.source_text: b. Accounting , reporting and communication responsibilities for which use of a Material Vendor  is prohibited (1 0/20/22) Freddie Mac requires that members of a Servicer's staff perform the most high -level accounting and reporting responsibilities.  As a result, the Servicer may not use a Material Vendor  to:  1. Review Borrower or Guarantor financial information or any protected personal information  2. Manage the Borrower relationship, including all communications with the Borrower, unless permitted otherwise by Section 50.2( a)  3. Communicate with Freddie Mac on behalf of the Servicer  4. Perform any services or functions that require or are likely to result in the Material Vendor receiving, using, handling, or otherwise having access to any financial or personal information pertaining to any Borrower, Guarantor, or other individual

examples: Servicer uses a vendor for cash management but prohibits vendor access to borrower statements containing PII; staff retains borrower financial review duties.
Vendor is asked to call borrowers about delinquent payments; this is prohibited borrower relationship management and must be performed by servicer staff or approved servicing agent.
Servicer wants vendor to upload remittance exceptions to Freddie Mac; prohibited because vendor cannot communicate with Freddie Mac on servicer’s behalf.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Seller/Servicer audit finding; required vendor scope change

examples: Seller/Servicer acting as Servicer for Freddie Mac-owned loans
Subservicer performing collections and reporting under oversight
Servicer implementing a remediation plan after reporting errors

dependencies.rationale: 026_010 prohibits using a Material Vendor for credit/performance risk decisions without Freddie Mac approval. 040_008 prohibits Material Vendors from performing accounting/reporting functions that expose borrower financial/PII or allow them to manage borrower relationships/communicate with Freddie. These rules are complementary: outsourcing governance must satisfy both sets of prohibitions depending on function type, and compliance navigation benefits from treating them as a linked control family.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If 026_010 is ignored while applying 040_008, the Servicer might still improperly delegate credit-risk decisions to a vendor. Conversely, focusing only on 026_010 could leave the Servicer exposed to PII/borrower-relationship delegation violations under 040_008.

business_rules.rule_name: Mortgage default definition under 46SBL.6(d)(1)
business_rules.description: During the Loss Obligation Period, a “Mortgage Default” occurs upon any event that entitles Freddie Mac to accelerate indebtedness or seek other relief against the Borrower under the Loan Documents (subject to any applicable grace or cure period). Upon Mortgage Default, the SBL Mortgage is a “Defaulted Mortgage.”
business_rules.conditions: Applies during the SBL Loss Obligation Period when default status is assessed
business_rules.consequences: If event qualifies, classify as Mortgage Default/Defaulted Mortgage for loss actions
business_rules.exceptions: Subject to any applicable grace or cure period in Loan Documents

source_reference.source_text: During the Loss Obligation Period for an SBL Mortgage, the occurrence of any event that entitles Freddie Mac to accelerate the indebtedness or seek other relief against the Borrower pursuant to the Loan Documents (subject to any applicable grace or cure period in the Loan Documents) is a “Mortgage Default,” and the SBL Mortgage is a “Defaulted Mortgage.”

examples: Borrower violates a covenant allowing acceleration under the Loan Documents → Mortgage Default; loan becomes a Defaulted Mortgage.
Payment default occurs, but note provides a cure period → still treated as Mortgage Default for status once the event entitles relief (subject to cure terms).
Freddie Mac can seek other relief (not just acceleration) under Loan Documents → still a Mortgage Default.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Default classification enabling loss calculation and remedies

examples: ACTIVE status where borrower payments are current
DELINQUENT status at 30 days delinquent
DEACTIVATED for accounting/reporting after specified servicing action

business_rules.rule_name: Floating-rate Note late charge 5% after 5-day grace period
business_rules.description: For a Floating-Rate Mortgage, the Note must provide that if an installment of principal (if applicable) and interest is received more than five days after its Due Date, a late charge accrues equal to 5% of the installment amount. If applicable law requires a longer/shorter minimum time period or a lower maximum late charge, the Seller must use the minimum time period and the maximum late charge permitted by applicable law. Late-charge provisions may not be changed without Freddie Mac’s prior approval.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to Floating-Rate Mortgage Notes under Chapter 17.3(f).
business_rules.consequences: If missing/noncompliant, Freddie Mac may require correction or reject purchase.
business_rules.exceptions: If state law limits timing/amount, use law-permitted minimum/maximum.

source_reference.source_text: The Floating -Rate Mortgage Note must provide that if an installment of principal (if applicable) and interest is received more than five days after its Due Date, a late charge will accrue equal to 5 percent of the amount of that installment. If applicable law prohibits imposition of a late charge until an installment is more than five days past due or provides for a maximum late charge of less than 5 percent, the Seller must use the minimum time period and the maximum late charge permitted by applicable law. The Seller/Servicer may not change any provisions regarding late charges without Freddie Mac's prior approval.

examples: Payment due 6/1 received 6/8: late charge = 5% of the 6/1 installment, unless state law caps it lower.
State law caps late charge at 4%: Note must use 4% (not 5%) and follow any statutory waiting period.
Seller changes late-charge clause post-closing without Freddie Mac approval: noncompliance requiring remediation.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Purchase refusal or corrective action demand
business_rules.extraction_notes: Explicit must-language with exact 5-day and 5% thresholds and law-limitation clause.

examples: Executed note with fixed interest rate and 30-year amortization
Modified note executed as part of a loan modification agreement
Note endorsed to Freddie Mac upon delivery

business_rules.rule_name: Default interest minimum Note rate plus 4% after 30 days past due
business_rules.description: For a Floating-Rate Mortgage, the Note must provide that if any installment remains past due for 30 days or more, or if the Borrower is in default under any other provision of the Note or Loan Documents, the Note bears default interest at a rate not less than 4% per annum in excess of the Note rate. If applicable law provides a lower maximum default interest rate, the Seller must use the maximum default interest permitted by applicable law.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when a Floating-Rate Mortgage installment is ≥30 days past due or other default.
business_rules.consequences: If noncompliant, Freddie Mac may require Note correction or reject purchase.
business_rules.exceptions: If state law caps default rate below Note+4%, use the law-permitted maximum.

source_reference.source_text: The Floating -Rate Mortgage Note must also provide that if any installment due under the Floating -Rate Mortgage Note remains past due for 30 days or more, or if the Borrower is in default under any other provision of the Floating -Rate Mortgage Note or Loan Documents, the Note will bear default interest at a rate that is not less than 4 percent per annum in excess of the Note rate. If applicable law provides for a maximum rate of default interest that is lower than 4 percent per annum in excess of the Floating-Rate Mortgage Note rate, the Seller must use the maximum rate of default interest permitted by applicable law.

examples: Note rate 6.25% and payment is 35 days past due: default interest must be at least 10.25%, unless capped by law.
Borrower violates a non-monetary covenant (other default): default interest trigger must apply per Note terms.
State law caps default interest at Note+3%: Seller must use the capped rate (not Note+4%).
business_rules.enforcement_action: Purchase refusal or corrective action demand
business_rules.extraction_notes: Explicit trigger (30 days) and minimum default rate (Note+4%) with legal cap exception.

examples: Executed note with fixed interest rate and 30-year amortization
Modified note executed as part of a loan modification agreement
Note endorsed to Freddie Mac upon delivery

business_rules.rule_name: Note endorsement must use Freddie Mac form and must not be dated
business_rules.description: The Note must bear an endorsement to Freddie Mac in the specified ‘PAY TO THE ORDER OF THE FEDERAL HOME LOAN MORTGAGE CORPORATION WITHOUT RECOURSE’ form, signed by a duly authorized officer (with typed name and title). The endorsement must not be dated. The endorsement may be on an allonge if the Freddie Mac loan number appears on the allonge and the allonge is attached to the Note. Any chain of endorsements must be complete from the original lender shown on the Note to Freddie Mac.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to Note endorsement in final delivery/assignment to Freddie Mac
business_rules.consequences: If compliant, Note endorsement supports enforceability and purchase; if noncompliant, delivery conditions/cure required and purchase may be delayed or denied
business_rules.exceptions: Allonge permitted if attached and loan number is shown; no other exceptions stated

source_reference.section_id: Chapter 32 – Forms for assignments and Note endorsement (10/01/25), Note Endorsement
source_reference.source_text: 2. Note Endorsement • The Note must bear an endorsement to Freddie Mac in the following form: PAY TO THE ORDER OF THE FEDERAL HOME LOAN MORTGAGE CORPORATION WITHOUT RECOURSE (Name of Seller-endorser) (Signature of duly authorized officer) (Typed name and title of signatory) • Do not date the Note endorsement. The Note endorsement may appear on an allonge to the Note if the Freddie Mac loan number appears on the allonge and the allonge is attached to the Note. • Any chain of endorsements must be complete from the original lender shown on the Note to Freddie Mac.

examples: Seller endorses the Note using the exact 'WITHOUT RECOURSE' language and leaves the endorsement undated; delivery is acceptable.
Seller places endorsement on an allonge but omits the Freddie Mac loan number on the allonge; Seller must correct before purchase.
Note shows an endorsement from originator to an interim warehouse bank, but no endorsement from warehouse bank to Freddie Mac; chain is incomplete and must be cured.
business_rules.extraction_notes: Highly explicit required endorsement language and prohibitions; numeric precision lower due to no numeric thresholds.

examples: Executed note with fixed interest rate and 30-year amortization
Modified note executed as part of a loan modification agreement
Note endorsed to Freddie Mac upon delivery

business_rules.rule_name: Early rate lock good faith deposit set at 1%–3% with $25k–$50k floors
business_rules.description: Unless the early rate lock application specifies otherwise, the standard early rate lock good faith deposit (GFD) amounts are: (1) Floating-rate loan: cash/letter of credit = greater of 1% of maximum Mortgage amount or $25,000; demand note = greater of 2% or $25,000. (2) Fixed-rate loan: cash/letter of credit with early rate lock 9 months or less = greater of 2% or $50,000; cash/letter of credit with lock >9 months and <12 months = greater of 3% or $50,000; demand note = greater of 3% or $50,000.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when using an early rate lock application and no custom GFD is set.
business_rules.consequences: If GFD is under-collected, Seller is noncompliant and may face remedies.
business_rules.exceptions: Different amount applies if specified in the early rate lock application.

source_reference.source_text: Unless otherwise set forth in the early rate lock application, the standard good faith deposit amounts are as follows: Floating -rate loan Cash or letter of credit Greater of 1% of the maximum Mortgage amount or $25,000 Demand note Greater of 2% of the maximum Mortgage amount or $25,000 Fixed -rate Loan Cash or letter of credit (Early Rate Lock 9 months or less) Greater of 2% of the maximum Mortgage amount or $50,000 Cash or letter of credit (Early Rate Lock > 9 months and < 12 months) Greater of 3% of the maximum Mortgage amount or $50,000 Demand note Greater of 3% of the maximum Mortgage amount or $50,000

examples: Floating-rate, max mortgage $3,000,000: cash GFD = greater of $30,000 (1%) or $25,000 → $30,000.
Fixed-rate, 8-month lock, max mortgage $1,500,000: cash GFD = greater of $30,000 (2%) or $50,000 → $50,000.
Fixed-rate, 10-month lock, max mortgage $5,000,000: cash GFD = greater of $150,000 (3%) or $50,000 → $150,000.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Contract remedies; potential breakage fee recovery
business_rules.extraction_notes: Table provides exact percentages, dollar floors, and lock-term conditions.

examples: New acquisition loan application for a multifamily property
Refinance loan application submitted via a delegated Seller/Servicer
Loan application declined triggering an adverse action notice

business_rules.rule_name: EPSH Form 1134 Must Be Executed for Program Benefits
business_rules.description: To qualify for the benefits described in the EPSH Fact Sheet, Form 1134 (Employer Enabled Permanent Supportive Housing Borrower Certification) must be used, must be executed by the Borrower, and must be provided with the full underwriting package. Form 1134 must include the number of EPSH units at the Property and the total annualized discounted rent for those units.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when loan seeks EPSH benefits under EPSH Fact Sheet eligibility
business_rules.consequences: If executed/submitted, loan may qualify for EPSH benefits; if not, EPSH benefits cannot be applied
business_rules.exceptions: None

source_reference.source_text: Employer Enabled Permanent Supportive Housing (EPSH) Borrower Certification, Form 1134 Form 1134, Employer Enabled Permanent Supportive Housing Borrower Certification , must be used to certify that the Borrower has a discounted employee unit/units at the Property that is/are subject to the EPSH Partnership Agreement (included as an exhibit to Form 1134 ). The Form 1134 will include the number of EPSH units at the Property as well as the total annualized discounted rent for those units. The Form 1134 must be executed by the Borrower and provided with the full underwriting package for a loan to qualify for the benefits described in the EPSH Fact Sheet .

examples: Borrower requests EPSH benefits and submits a Borrower-executed Form 1134 showing 12 EPSH units and $96,000 total annualized discounted rent; underwriting package is complete for EPSH consideration.
Borrower requests EPSH benefits but provides an unsigned Form 1134; EPSH benefits are not available until a fully executed form is provided.
Borrower requests EPSH benefits but submits an alternative certification form instead of Form 1134; Seller must cure by using Form 1134 to qualify.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loss of program benefits; underwriting package deficiency
business_rules.extraction_notes: Mandatory 'must' language is explicit; numeric content is structural (counts/annualized rent) rather than a threshold.

examples: New acquisition loan application for a multifamily property
Refinance loan application submitted via a delegated Seller/Servicer
Loan application declined triggering an adverse action notice

business_rules.rule_name: EPSH Partnership Agreement Must Be Exhibit and Quantify Discount
business_rules.description: For a loan to qualify for EPSH benefits, the Seller must provide a fully executed EPSH Partnership Agreement between the Sponsor and a Nonprofit Entity as an exhibit to Form 1134. The agreement should quantify the rent discount to be offered if a candidate is hired.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when Form 1134 is used to pursue EPSH benefits for the loan
business_rules.consequences: If provided/executed, supports EPSH eligibility; if missing, EPSH benefits cannot be applied
business_rules.exceptions: None

source_reference.section_id: Guide Chapter 55 – Documentation and Deliveries (EPSH Partnership Agreement)
source_reference.source_text: Employer Enabled Permanent Supportive Housing (EPSH) Partnership Agreement A fully executed agreement between the Sponsor and a Nonprofit Entity to offer discounted units to employees sourced via the Nonprofit Entity’s job matching network for those at risk of homelessness without subsidy or financial incentive to the Sponsor. The agreement should quantify the discount to be offered if a candidate is hired and must be provided as an exhibit to Form 1134, Employer Enabled Permanent Supportive Housing Borrower Certification , for a loan to qualify for the benefits described in the EPSH Fact Sheet .

examples: Sponsor and Nonprofit execute an agreement stating a $250/month discount per hired candidate; Seller includes it as an exhibit to Form 1134 in the full underwriting package.
Seller submits Form 1134 but omits the executed Partnership Agreement exhibit; the loan cannot qualify for EPSH benefits until cured.
Agreement is included but does not quantify the discount; Seller must obtain an updated agreement (or addendum) that quantifies the discount for EPSH qualification.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loss of program benefits; underwriting package deficiency
business_rules.extraction_notes: Mandatory exhibit requirement is explicit; 'should quantify' introduces minor discretion but is still a clear expectation tied to eligibility.

examples: New acquisition loan application for a multifamily property
Refinance loan application submitted via a delegated Seller/Servicer
Loan application declined triggering an adverse action notice

dependencies.rationale: 033_003 states the Seller must provide a fully executed EPSH Partnership Agreement 'as an exhibit to Form 1134'. 033_002 requires Form 1134 be executed by the Borrower and provided with the underwriting package to qualify for EPSH benefits. Therefore, completion/submission of Form 1134 is a necessary prerequisite to attaching the exhibit required by 033_003.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If 033_002 fails (Form 1134 not executed/submitted), 033_003 cannot be complied with as written and EPSH benefits cannot be applied.

business_rules.rule_name: TIC borrower limited to 10 owners and each owner must be an SPE
business_rules.description: A tenancy-in-common (TIC) may be an eligible Borrower only if: (1) there are not more than 10 TIC owners, and (2) each TIC owner is a Single Purpose Entity (SPE) regardless of loan size.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when Borrower ownership structure is a tenancy-in-common (TIC).
business_rules.consequences: If >10 owners or any owner not an SPE, TIC Borrower is not eligible.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated.

source_reference.source_text: 9.12 Tenancy -in-common (TIC) ( 04/15/21 ) A TIC may be an eligible Borrower if it meets all of the following conditions. a. Conditions applicable to TIC owners (06/30/15) • There may not be more than 10 TIC owners. • Each TIC owner must be a Single Purpose Entity (SPE) regardless of the size of the loan.

examples: TIC with 8 owners, each an SPE LLC: eligible (subject to other Guide requirements).
TIC with 11 owners: ineligible due to exceeding the 10-owner maximum.
TIC with 6 owners but one owner is an individual (not an SPE): ineligible.
business_rules.extraction_notes: Explicit hard cap (10) and entity-type requirement (SPE).

examples: Single-asset entity (SAE) borrower owning a 150-unit property
Limited partnership borrower with a managing member
Borrower under a loan modification during servicing

business_rules.rule_name: Foreign guarantor without U.S. guarantor must hold 5% liquidity and provide opinions at $40M/$50M
business_rules.description: If there is no additional financially compliant United States Guarantor and Freddie Mac approves a Foreign Guarantor, the Foreign Guarantor must: (1) maintain minimum liquidity in one or more U.S. bank accounts of 5% of the original principal balance of the Mortgage (may be held in aggregate across multiple Foreign Guarantors) with banks acceptable to Freddie Mac; (2) for any Mortgage > $40 million, provide an additional non-consolidation opinion acceptable to Freddie Mac from the Foreign Guarantor’s country of citizenship/residency (written in English); and (3) for any Mortgage > $50 million, deliver an English opinion of counsel addressing enforceability of process agent appointment and procedures to collect/enforce U.S. judgments in the Foreign Guarantor’s country (counsel/firm acceptable to Freddie Mac).
business_rules.conditions: Applies when Freddie Mac-approved Foreign Guarantor lacks an additional compliant U.S. guarantor.
business_rules.consequences: If unmet, Freddie Mac may deem structure noncompliant and require cure or deny purchase.
business_rules.exceptions: Liquidity may be held in aggregate if more than one Foreign Guarantor.

source_reference.section_id: Guide Chapter 9 (Foreign Guarantor requirements excerpt on Page 17)
source_reference.source_text: If there is no additional financially compliant United States Guarantor, the Foreign Guarantor will be required to: • Maintain a minimum Liquidity in one or more U.S. bank account(s) of five percent of the original principal balance of the Mortgage... • For any Mortgage greater than $40 million, provide an additional non -consolidation opinion... • For any Mortgage greater than $50 million, deliver an opinion of counsel... covering (i) the validity and enforceability of the form of appointment of the process agent... and (ii) the procedure for the collection and enforcement of any U.S. judgment...

examples: Mortgage OPB $30,000,000 with only foreign guarantor: must evidence ≥$1,500,000 liquidity in acceptable U.S. bank account(s).
Mortgage $45,000,000: must provide the additional non-consolidation opinion (English) from foreign jurisdiction counsel.
Mortgage $60,000,000: must provide both the >$40M non-consolidation opinion and the >$50M process-agent/judgment-enforcement opinion.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Purchase refusal or additional conditions imposed
business_rules.extraction_notes: Clear numeric thresholds (5%, $40M, $50M); effective date inferred from bulletin header on the same chunk.

examples: Single-asset entity (SAE) borrower owning a 150-unit property
Limited partnership borrower with a managing member
Borrower under a loan modification during servicing

business_rules.rule_name: Qualifying Manager requires 5 years and up to 10,000 units
business_rules.description: Where a TIC Agreement requires a property manager, the Manager must be a "Qualifying Manager" meeting all stated criteria: reputable management company with at least 5 years’ experience in managing multifamily properties in the relevant geographic area; for at least 5 years prior to engagement has managed at least 5 multifamily properties; at engagement manages same property type units equal to the lesser of 10,000 units or 5× the Property’s unit count; and is not subject to bankruptcy or similar insolvency proceeding.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when Borrower structure uses TIC Agreement requiring a Manager
business_rules.consequences: If not met, Freddie Mac may require replacement manager or deem structure noncompliant
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.section_id: Guide Chapter 9 – Borrower/Borrower Principal Fundamentals; TIC Agreement – Management
source_reference.source_text: A “Qualifying Manager” is a property manager acceptable to Lender which meets all of the following requirements: o Is a reputable management company having at least five years’ experience in the management of multifamily properties and in the metropolitan area or other appropriate geographic area in which the Property is located o Has, for at least five years prior to its engagement as property manager, managed at least five multifamily properties o At the time of its engagement as property manager, has units of the same property type as the Property equal to the lesser of (A) 10,000 units and (B) five times the number of units the Property has o Is not the subject of a bankruptcy or similar insolvency proceeding

examples: Property has 200 units; manager must manage same-type units ≥ 1,000 (5×200) at engagement and meet 5-year experience criteria.
Property has 3,000 units; 5× would be 15,000 but cap applies, so manager must manage ≥ 10,000 units of same type.
Proposed manager has 6 years in market but has managed only 3 multifamily properties in prior 5 years; manager is not a Qualifying Manager and must be replaced.
business_rules.extraction_notes: Definition-style eligibility criteria with multiple explicit numeric thresholds (5 years, 5 properties, 10,000 units, 5× units).
business_rules.enforcement_action: Freddie Mac purchase ineligibility until cured

examples: Single-asset entity (SAE) borrower owning a 150-unit property
Limited partnership borrower with a managing member
Borrower under a loan modification during servicing

dependencies.rationale: 003_009 establishes when a tenancy-in-common (TIC) borrower structure is eligible (<=10 owners; each owner SPE). 006_004 applies 'where a TIC Agreement requires a property manager' and defines Qualifying Manager criteria. In workflow terms, once a deal is identified as TIC (and proceeds under TIC eligibility), the Servicer/Seller must evaluate the manager qualification gate if the TIC agreement requires a manager.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If 003_009 TIC context is not correctly identified/validated, 006_004 may be missed, leading to an unqualified manager under a TIC arrangement and resulting in required replacement or noncompliance.

business_rules.rule_name: SBL guarantor average FICO score minimum 680
business_rules.description: For SBL Borrower Principal/Guarantor that is an individual, the individual must have an average FICO score of 680 or better across all three national credit bureaus (Equifax, TransUnion, Experian). If fewer than three FICO scores are available, then all available FICO scores must be 680 or better.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when an SBL Borrower Principal/Guarantor is an individual.
business_rules.consequences: Meets threshold: eligible on FICO; fails: Freddie may not approve principal/guarantor.
business_rules.exceptions: If fewer than 3 scores exist, all available scores must be ≥680.

source_reference.section_id: Guide Chapter 9SBL – SBL Borrower/Borrower Principal Fundamentals (Guide Bulletin Update 12/16/25)
source_reference.source_text: • If an individual, must have an average FICO® score of 680 or better with all three national credit bureaus (Equifax, TransUnion or Experian) . If less than three FICO scores are available, all FICO scores must be 680 or better

examples: Guarantor has Equifax 690, TransUnion 675, Experian 685 (avg 683.3): meets the 680 average requirement.
Guarantor has only two scores: 679 and 702: fails because not all available scores are ≥680.
Guarantor has three scores 720, 710, 700 (avg 710): meets and can proceed on this criterion.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Refusal to approve Borrower Principal/Guarantor; purchase ineligibility

examples: Single-asset entity (SAE) borrower owning a 150-unit property
Limited partnership borrower with a managing member
Borrower under a loan modification during servicing

business_rules.rule_name: Foreign guarantor U.S. bank liquidity minimum 5% OPB
business_rules.description: When there is no financially compliant U.S. Guarantor, the Foreign Guarantor(s) must maintain minimum Liquidity in one or more U.S. bank accounts equal to 5% of the original principal balance of the SBL Mortgage (or such other amount as Freddie Mac may require). The bank(s) holding the account must be acceptable to Freddie Mac, and the requirement may be satisfied in the aggregate by the Foreign Guarantor(s), exclusive of any U.S. Guarantor liquidity.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when no financially compliant U.S. Guarantor exists for an SBL Mortgage.
business_rules.consequences: Meets liquidity: condition satisfied; fails: Freddie may deny approval of Foreign Guarantor.
business_rules.exceptions: Freddie may require an amount other than 5%; banks must be acceptable.

source_reference.section_id: Guide Chapter 9SBL – Foreign Borrower Principal/Foreign Guarantor (02/22/24)
source_reference.source_text: o Maintain a minimum Liquidity in one or more U.S. bank accounts equal to five percent of the original principal balance of the SBL Mortgage, or such other amount as Freddie Mac may require; the bank(s) holding the account must be acceptable to Freddie Mac . This requirement may be satisfied in the aggregate by the Foreign Guarantor (s) exclusive of any U.S. Guarantor Liquidity.

examples: Original principal balance is $8,000,000; required U.S. bank liquidity is at least $400,000 across acceptable U.S. banks.
Two foreign guarantors hold $250,000 and $200,000 in U.S. bank accounts (total $450,000) for a $8,000,000 loan: meets in aggregate.
Foreign guarantor holds sufficient cash in a non-U.S. bank only: fails because liquidity must be in one or more U.S. bank accounts acceptable to Freddie.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Refusal to approve Foreign Guarantor; purchase/commitment denial

examples: Single-asset entity (SAE) borrower owning a 150-unit property
Limited partnership borrower with a managing member
Borrower under a loan modification during servicing

business_rules.rule_name: No purchase if borrower matches OFAC or FHFA lists
business_rules.description: Freddie Mac will not purchase a Mortgage, allow a Transfer of Ownership under Chapter 41SBL, or approve other servicing-related transactions if it determines the Borrower, Borrower Principal, or Guarantor is a confirmed match to specified sanctions/exclusion lists (OFAC SDN, OFAC Consolidated Sanctions, FHFA Suspended Counterparty List subject to suspension order conditions, or Freddie Mac Exclusionary List).
business_rules.conditions: Applies to any borrower/principal/guarantor in SBL purchase, TO, or servicing approvals.
business_rules.consequences: Confirmed match: Freddie will not purchase/approve; no match: may proceed if other criteria met.
business_rules.exceptions: FHFA list applies subject to conditions/exclusions in final suspension order.

source_reference.source_text: Freddie Mac will not purchase any Mortgage or allow any Transfer of Ownership under Chapter 41SBL, or approve any other Servicing -related transaction if it determines that any of the following is true with regard to a Borrower, Borrower Principal, or Guarantor... 1. Is a confirmed match to a name on any of the following lists: • OFAC Specially Designated Nationals and Blocked Persons List • OFAC Consolidated Sanctions List • FHFA Suspended Counterparty List ... • Freddie Mac Exclusionary List

examples: Borrower principal name is confirmed match to OFAC SDN: Freddie will not purchase the Mortgage.
Guarantor is on Freddie Mac Exclusionary List: transfer of ownership request is denied.
Name resembles an OFAC entry but is not a confirmed match after screening: this rule is not triggered.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan purchase denial; refusal to approve servicing-related transaction

examples: Single-asset entity (SAE) borrower owning a 150-unit property
Limited partnership borrower with a managing member
Borrower under a loan modification during servicing

dependencies.rationale: BR_018_009 requires the Seller/Servicer to use the most current Exclusionary List and states that if a listed party played certain roles, the mortgage is not eligible. BR_007_003 states Freddie Mac will not purchase/approve if Borrower/Borrower Principal/Guarantor matches sanctions/exclusion lists including the Freddie Mac Exclusionary List. Practically, BR_018_009 is the screening mechanism that provides the factual input (match/no match) necessary to apply BR_007_003’s prohibition decision for borrower/principal/guarantor.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_018_009 fails (outdated list or no screening): loans may be submitted with prohibited parties, leading to purchase denial, TO/servicing approval denial, pipeline fallout, and potential regulatory/sanctions exposure. Conversely, using stale data can cause false clears or false hits that disrupt processing.

business_rules.rule_name: SPE Required Unless Loan Under $5 Million Requested
business_rules.description: Each Borrower and each SPE Equity Owner (if applicable) must be a Single Purpose Entity (SPE), and if the Borrower is a TIC, each tenant must be an SPE. Exception: for each Mortgage with an initial principal balance of less than $5 million, at the Borrower’s request (except for TICs), Freddie Mac will not require the Borrower to be an SPE. SPEs must be newly formed, but if the Mortgage has an initial principal balance of $100,000,000 or less, Freddie Mac may accept a recycled SPE if the commitment/application recycled SPE requirements are met.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to borrower entity structure for Freddie Mac multifamily Mortgages
business_rules.consequences: If not satisfied, Borrower structure may be ineligible or require Freddie Mac approval
business_rules.exceptions: Non-TIC loans < $5M may waive SPE at Borrower request; recycled SPE allowed ≤$100M

source_reference.source_text: Each Borrower and each SPE Equity Owner (if applicable) must be a Single Purpose Entity (SPE). If the Borrower is a TIC, each tenant must be an SPE. However, for each Mortgage with an initial principal balance of less than $5 million, at the request of the Borrower (except for TICs), Freddie Mac will not require the Borrower to be an SPE. Each SPE Borrower and each SPE Equity Owner (if applicable) must be newly formed; provided, however, that if a Mortgage has an initial principal balance of $100,000,000 or less, Freddie Mac may accept a recycled SPE if all recycled SPE requirements set forth in the Letter of Commitment or early rate lock application are met.

examples: Loan initial principal balance is $4,750,000 and Borrower requests non-SPE structure (not a TIC): Freddie Mac will not require SPE for the Borrower.
Loan initial principal balance is $6,000,000: Borrower must be an SPE (unless another Guide provision applies).
Loan initial principal balance is $90,000,000 and Borrower proposes a recycled SPE: permitted only if recycled SPE requirements in the commitment/early rate-lock application are met.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Purchase suspension/decline until compliant borrower structure is provided

examples: Single-asset entity (SAE) borrower owning a 150-unit property
Limited partnership borrower with a managing member
Borrower under a loan modification during servicing

business_rules.rule_name: Seniors housing borrower needs 10 years and 500 units
business_rules.description: For Freddie Mac Multifamily Seniors Housing Mortgages, a Seniors Housing Borrower or Key Borrower Principal must demonstrate eligibility by meeting all criteria: (1) own or manage comparable facilities in a similar property type in similar or adjoining markets, (2) have ten or more years' ownership or management experience, and (3) own or manage a minimum of five properties with at least 500 units.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to Seniors Housing Mortgages during underwriting review
business_rules.consequences: Meets criteria: borrower eligible; violates: mortgage not eligible for purchase
business_rules.exceptions: None stated; Freddie Mac discretion may apply to mixed services

source_reference.source_text: c. Eligible Borrower (0 4/13/23 ) A Seniors Housing Borrower or Key Borrower Principal must demonstrate eligibility by meeting all of the following criteria:  • Own or manage comparable facilities in a similar property type (Independent Living Property, Assisted Living Residence, Skilled Nursing Property or CCRC) in similar or adjoining markets  • Have ten or more years' ownership or management experience  • Own or manage a minimum of five properties with at least 500 units

examples: Borrower principal has 12 years managing assisted living and owns 6 properties totaling 650 units: eligible.
Borrower principal has 15 years experience but owns only 3 properties totaling 420 units: ineligible.
Borrower has 10+ years and 5 properties/500+ units but none in similar/adjoining markets: ineligible.
Single-asset entity (SAE) borrower owning a 150-unit property
Limited partnership borrower with a managing member
Borrower under a loan modification during servicing

business_rules.rule_name: Non-profit Sponsor Needs 3 Years and 3 Properties Experience
business_rules.description: When a non-profit entity is the Borrower or Key Borrower Principal, it must have a minimum of three years’ experience in the same capacity it will have for the proposed transaction and must have experience acquiring, developing, or owning a minimum of three properties.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when a non-profit entity is Borrower or Key Borrower Principal
business_rules.consequences: Meets rule: non-profit experience requirement satisfied; violated: not eligible without Freddie Mac risk mitigation/approval
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in cited text

source_reference.section_id: Guide Chapter 9, Section 9.2(f) (as shown on Chapter 9 Page 6-7 update 04/21/26)
source_reference.source_text: The following requirements apply when a non-profit entity is the Borrower or the Key Borrower Principal: • The non-profit Borrower/ Key Borrower Principal must have a minimum of three years’ experience o In the same capacity that it will have for the proposed transaction, and o Acquiring, developing or owning a minimum of three properties.

examples: A non-profit property owner with 7 years owning 5 LIHTC properties qualifies on experience.
A non-profit formed 18 months ago with one acquired property fails the 3-year/3-property minimum and is not eligible without Freddie Mac direction.
A non-profit with 10 years as a service provider but only 1 year as an owner fails the “same capacity” requirement for an ownership role.
business_rules.extraction_notes: Explicit numeric minimums (3 years, 3 properties) with clear applicability to non-profit borrower/principal.

examples: Single-asset entity (SAE) borrower owning a 150-unit property
Limited partnership borrower with a managing member
Borrower under a loan modification during servicing

business_rules.rule_name: No Lead Actions If Built 1978+ or Below 0.5%
business_rules.description: Freddie Mac does not require specific Borrower actions for lead-based paint (LBP) if the Property was built in 1978 or later, OR a qualified environmental professional issued a “lead free” certificate (reviewed by the environmental consultant), OR properly specified testing shows acceptable LBP levels (<0.5% by weight by laboratory analysis OR 1.0 mg/cm2 by XRF) AND the Property satisfies State and local LBP laws.
business_rules.conditions: LBP review during environmental due diligence for a multifamily Property
business_rules.consequences: If conditions met, no Borrower LBP action required; if not met, O&M/remediation may be required
business_rules.exceptions: None (this rule itself defines the exception cases)

source_reference.section_id: Chapter 61 – Environmental Requirements; Issue resolution by Borrower (02/16/23)
source_reference.source_text: Freddie Mac does not require any specific Borrower actions if  • The Property was built in 1978 or later, or  • A “lead free” certificate has been issued by a qualified environmental professional in accordance with EPA, HUD or State requirements, and the environmental consultant has reviewed all supporting documentation, or  • All painted surfaces have been properly tested as specified in Section 61. 11(a) and found to contain acceptable levels of LBP (less than 0.5 percent by weight as measured by laboratory analysis or 1.0 mg/cm2 as measured by XRF), and  • The Property satisfies State and local LBP laws.

examples: A 1995-built property: Borrower provides build year evidence; no LBP O&M or remediation required.
A 1965-built property with lab results showing 0.3% lead by weight and compliance with local law: no additional Borrower action required.
A 1972-built property with a qualified “lead free” certificate reviewed by the consultant: no LBP action required.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Purchase eligibility condition / underwriting hold until LBP requirements satisfied

examples: Single-asset entity (SAE) borrower owning a 150-unit property
Limited partnership borrower with a managing member
Borrower under a loan modification during servicing

business_rules.rule_name: Pre-1978 Untested Paint Requires LBP O&M Program
business_rules.description: For untested Properties built prior to 1978 and for Properties with documented lead-based paint (LBP), the Borrower must maintain all painted surfaces according to an Operations & Maintenance (O&M) program meeting Section 61.11(f) requirements.
business_rules.conditions: Property built before 1978 AND (untested OR documented LBP present)
business_rules.consequences: If met, loan can proceed with O&M; if violated, Freddie may require O&M/remediation as a condition to purchase
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.section_id: Chapter 61 – Environmental Requirements; Issue resolution by Borrower (02/16/23)
source_reference.source_text: For untested Properties built prior to 1978 and for Properties with documented LBP, the Borrower must maintain all painted surfaces according to an O&M program that meets the requirements set forth in Section 61. 11(f).

examples: A 1968 property has no LBP testing performed: Borrower must implement an LBP O&M program meeting Section 61.11(f).
A 1955 property has an older report documenting LBP: Borrower must operate under an LBP O&M program for the Mortgage term.
A 1979 property: this pre-1978 O&M requirement does not apply.
Single-asset entity (SAE) borrower owning a 150-unit property
Limited partnership borrower with a managing member
Borrower under a loan modification during servicing

business_rules.rule_name: LBP O&M Training Requires 1 Day and Certification
business_rules.description: An LBP O&M program must provide for training of maintenance staff, supervisors, and property managers who may perform O&M tasks. Maintenance supervisors and property managers must attend at least one day of LBP training and must maintain certification of successful course completion. Training must meet one of the listed program standards (EPA/HUD model curriculum, EPA/State accreditation for abatement workers/supervisors, or sponsorship by an appropriate State/local agency).
business_rules.conditions: LBP O&M program is required for the Property under Section 61.11(f)
business_rules.consequences: If met, Borrower satisfies O&M training element; if violated, O&M program is deficient and must be corrected
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.source_text: a. The program must provide for training of all maintenance staff persons, supervisors and property managers who may perform any of the tasks included in the O&M program. Maintenance supervisors and property managers must attend at least one day of LBP training and must maintain certification of successful course completion.  The trainees may complete any training program that meets the following requirements:  • Employs an EPA - or U.S. Department of Housing and Urban Development (HUD) -model training curriculum  • Is accredited by the EPA or the State for training abatement workers and/or supervisors  • Is sponsored by an appropriate State or local agency

examples: A 1962 property requires O&M: maintenance supervisors attend a 1-day EPA-accredited LBP course and retain completion certificates in the O&M records.
A property manager performs O&M-related oversight without any LBP course completion certificate: Borrower must train and document certification to cure the deficiency.
Custodial staff working under O&M are trained using an HUD-model curriculum sponsored by a city health department: training requirement is satisfied.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Servicing/underwriting corrective action; potential noncompliance fee

examples: Single-asset entity (SAE) borrower owning a 150-unit property
Limited partnership borrower with a managing member
Borrower under a loan modification during servicing

business_rules.rule_name: LBP Re-inspect Each Unit Annually with Wet Wipe
business_rules.description: The property manager must establish a re-inspection schedule for the term of the Mortgage that includes a re-inspection of each living unit at least annually. At each re-inspection, the inspector must perform a thorough wet wipe-down of all horizontal surfaces, even if painted surfaces are in excellent condition.
business_rules.conditions: Property is operating under an LBP O&M program for the Mortgage term
business_rules.consequences: If met, O&M re-inspection element satisfied; if violated, Borrower must perform/restore annual inspections and cleaning
business_rules.exceptions: None stated (applies even if paint is in excellent condition)

source_reference.source_text: c. The property manager must establish a re -inspection schedule for the term of the Mortgage. The schedule must include a re -inspection of each living unit at least annually.  The inspector must perform a thorough wet wipe -down of all horizontal surfaces at each re -inspection, even if the painted surfaces are in excellent condition.

examples: An O&M property schedules unit re-inspections every 12 months and documents wet wipe-down completion in O&M logs: compliant.
A property performs re-inspections every 24 months: noncompliant; must adjust schedule to at least annual and catch up inspections.
A property re-inspects annually but skips wet wipe-down when paint looks intact: noncompliant because wipe-down is required even in excellent condition.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Servicing corrective action; potential noncompliance fee

examples: Single-asset entity (SAE) borrower owning a 150-unit property
Limited partnership borrower with a managing member
Borrower under a loan modification during servicing

business_rules.rule_name: Active UST tightness test required unless within 6 months
business_rules.description: For any active underground storage tank (UST), the Borrower must obtain a tightness test to assess the stability of the tank vessel and determine if there are any active leaks. Testing must comply with ASTM standards and applicable State law. Freddie Mac may waive the tightness test if an acceptable tightness test was performed within six months of the property inspection or if an acceptable, documented release detection system is in place.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when the Property has any active underground storage tank (UST)
business_rules.consequences: If test/waiver condition satisfied, environmental condition is met; if not, UST condition remains unresolved
business_rules.exceptions: Waiver if acceptable test within 6 months or release detection system exists

source_reference.section_id: Chapter 61 – Environmental Requirements, Issue resolution by Borrower (02/16/23)
source_reference.source_text: For any active UST, the Borrower must obtain a tightness test to assess the stability of the tank vessel and determine if there are any active leaks. The manner of testing must comply with ASTM standards and applicable State law. Freddie Mac may waive the tightness test if an acceptable tightness test has been performed within six months of the date of the property inspection or if an acceptable, documented release detection system is in place in accordance with Section 61. 6(b).

examples: Property inspection on 2026-05-01; last acceptable tightness test on 2026-02-15 (within 6 months): Freddie Mac may waive the new test if documentation is provided.
Active UST has no test in past 6 months and no documented release detection system: Borrower must obtain a tightness test compliant with ASTM and State law.
Active UST has documented secondary containment with interstitial monitoring meeting requirements: Borrower may qualify for waiver instead of a new tightness test.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan conditions/holdbacks until environmental requirement satisfied

examples: Single-asset entity (SAE) borrower owning a 150-unit property
Limited partnership borrower with a managing member
Borrower under a loan modification during servicing

business_rules.rule_name: Elevated seismic hazard region defined as PGA at least 0.15g
business_rules.description: An Elevated Seismic Hazard Region is a geographic location with horizontal Peak Ground Acceleration (PGA) ≥ 0.15g. For continental U.S. properties, the PGA must be calculated using the USGS website. For properties in Hawaii, Alaska (within 100 miles of Anchorage or the Gulf of Alaska), or U.S. territories, the Seller/Servicer must assume PGA > 0.15g. For areas of Alaska outside the delineated area, Seller/Servicer can assume PGA < 0.15g.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when determining if a property is in Elevated Seismic Hazard Region
business_rules.consequences: Determines whether seismic risk documentation evaluation is triggered
business_rules.exceptions: Alaska outside delineated area may assume PGA < 0.15g

source_reference.section_id: 64SBL.2(b) Determining whether a Property is located in an Elevated Seismic Hazard Region (12/15/22)
source_reference.source_text: An “Elevated Seismic Hazard Region” is defined as a geographic location with a horizontal Peak Ground Acceleration (PGA) equal to or greater than 0.15g. For Properties located in the continental United States, the PGA must be calculated using the United States Geological Survey (USGS) website. For any Property located in Hawaii, Alaska (limited to within 100 miles of the city of Anchorage or the Gulf of Alaska), or one of the U.S. territories, the Seller/Servicer must assume that the PGA is greater than 0.15g

examples: Property in Los Angeles, CA: Seller/Servicer calculates PGA via USGS; if PGA is 0.16g, the property is in an Elevated Seismic Hazard Region.
Property in Honolulu, HI: Seller/Servicer must assume PGA > 0.15g (no USGS calculation needed for the threshold determination).
Property in Alaska 200 miles from Anchorage and outside the delineated area: Seller/Servicer may assume PGA < 0.15g, so Elevated Seismic Hazard Region definition is not met.
business_rules.extraction_notes: Clear numeric definition (0.15g) and explicit geographic assumptions.

examples: Environmental site assessment report used in underwriting
Property condition assessment report required for delivery
Engineering report triggering repair escrow requirements

business_rules.rule_name: Level 1 SRA requires Form 1102 and USGS PDF submission
business_rules.description: A Level 1 seismic investigation (Level 1 SRA) is required for a property located in an Elevated Seismic Hazard Region that exhibits any seismic risk factors; if required, the Consultant must complete and submit Form 1102, Seismic Risk Assessment Summary, with each SRA. When a Level 1 SRA is required, the Seller/Servicer must obtain an SRA meeting Chapter 64SBL requirements and upload the USGS output PDF into DMS and submit the Level 1 SRA and the PDF to Freddie Mac in the applicable underwriting package as required seismic risk documentation.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when Level 1 SRA is required under Chapter 64SBL
business_rules.consequences: If submitted, underwriting package includes required seismic docs; if not, docs deficient
business_rules.exceptions: SRA not required if not in Elevated Region or no risk factors present

source_reference.source_text: A Level 1 SRA is required for a Property that is located in an Elevated Seismic Hazard Region, as defined in Section 64SBL.2(a), and that exhibits any of the seismic risk factors identified in Section 64SBL.2(c). If a Level 1 SRA is required, then the Consultant must complete and submit Form 1102, Seismic Risk Assessment Summary, with each SRA.

examples: PGA is 0.20g and property has a listed seismic risk factor: obtain Level 1 SRA and include Form 1102 with the SRA submission.
PGA is 0.16g and Seller/Servicer cannot conclusively determine risk factors are absent: treat as risk factors potentially present and obtain Level 1 SRA with Form 1102.
Property is outside Elevated Seismic Hazard Region: do not order SRA; do not submit Form 1102.
business_rules.extraction_notes: Direct 'required' language for Level 1 SRA and Form 1102; USGS PDF upload requirement appears later in same section but is part of the same documented process.

examples: Environmental site assessment report used in underwriting
Property condition assessment report required for delivery
Engineering report triggering repair escrow requirements

business_rules.rule_name: Elevated Seismic Hazard Region Defined as PGA at 0.15g
business_rules.description: An Elevated Seismic Hazard Region is a geographic location with horizontal Peak Ground Acceleration (PGA) ≥ 0.15g. For continental U.S. properties, PGA must be calculated using the USGS website with a 10% in 50 years (475 years) return period. For Hawaii, Alaska (within 100 miles of Anchorage or the Gulf of Alaska), and U.S. territories, the Seller/Servicer must assume PGA > 0.15g and an SRA is required; for Alaska outside the delineated area, Seller/Servicer can assume PGA < 0.15g and no SRA is required.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when determining whether seismic risk documentation/SRA is required
business_rules.consequences: If PGA ≥ 0.15g, SRA required; if < 0.15g, no seismic risk documentation required
business_rules.exceptions: Hawaii/covered Alaska/territories assumed >0.15g; Alaska outside area assumed <0.15g

source_reference.source_text: An “Elevated Seismic Hazard Region” is defined as a geographic location with a horizontal Peak Ground Acceleration (PGA) equal to or greater than 0.15g. For Properties located in the continental United States, the PGA must be calculated using the United States Geological Survey (USGS) website. For any Property located in Hawaii, Alaska (limited to within 100 miles of the city of Anchorage or the Gulf of Alaska), or one of the U.S. territories, the Seller/Servicer must assume that the PGA is greater than 0.15g, and an SRA is required. For areas of Alaska outside the delineated area, the Seller/Servicer can assume a PGA of less than 0.15g, and therefore an SRA is not required. Time Horizon /Earthquake Return Period 10% in 50 years (475 years)

examples: Property in Los Angeles, CA has USGS PGA = 0.21g at 10% in 50 years: property is in an Elevated Seismic Hazard Region and requires an SRA.
Property in Texas has USGS PGA = 0.08g: SRA is not required under Chapter 64.
Property in Hawaii: Seller/Servicer must assume PGA > 0.15g and obtain an SRA without performing the USGS PGA calculation.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Purchase condition/hold pending required seismic documentation

business_rules.rule_name: Non-SBL survey must be dated within 90 days of the Note
business_rules.description: For each non-SBL Mortgage purchased by Freddie Mac, the Seller/Servicer must submit a survey meeting the then-current minimum standard detail requirements for ALTA/NSPS Land Title Surveys. The survey must be made, dated, or revised by a licensed civil engineer or registered surveyor not more than 90 days prior to the date of the Note.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to non-SBL mortgages where a survey is required for purchase/delivery
business_rules.consequences: Compliant survey: delivery eligible; stale/noncompliant survey: delivery condition or rejection
business_rules.exceptions: SBL mortgages: new survey not required (separate rule/section)

source_reference.source_text: 1. For each Mortgage purchased by Freddie Mac, the Seller /Servicer  must submit a survey meeting the then -current minimum standard detail requirements for American Land Title Association/National Society of Professional Surveyors, Inc. (ALTA/NSPS) Land Title Surveys.  The survey must be made, dated or revised by a licensed civil engineer or registered surveyor not more than 90 days prior to the date of the Note.

Note date 2026-06-30 and survey dated 2026-03-15 (107 days prior): noncompliant; new/revised survey required.
Survey dated within 90 days but not an ALTA/NSPS survey: noncompliant until corrected.
