business_rules.rule_name: LBP testing not required for properties built 1978 or later
business_rules.description: For environmental assessments, the environmental consultant is not required to test for lead-based paint (LBP) in a Property built in 1978 or later. A Property built before 1978 is considered to contain LBP unless testing proves otherwise; if the original certificate of occupancy (or legal equivalent) was issued before January 1, 1978, Freddie Mac treats the Property as built before 1978 (pre-1978 Property).
business_rules.conditions: When determining LBP testing scope based on Property build year/COO date
business_rules.consequences: If met: correct LBP scope applied; if violated: report may be deficient
business_rules.exceptions: Pre-1978 may test or presume LBP and implement O&M program

source_reference.source_text: The environmental consultant is not required to test for LBP in a Property built in 1978 or later. A Property built before 1978 is considered to contain LBP unless testing proves otherwise. If the Property's original certificate of occupancy or legal equivalent was issued before January 1, 1978, Freddie Mac considers the Property to be built before 1978 (pre-1978 Property).

examples: Property built in 1985: environmental report may omit LBP testing and still comply with minimum scope.
Property built in 1965: treated as LBP-present unless testing proves otherwise; consultant must address paint condition.
COO issued in 1977 though renovations occurred in 1982: treated as pre-1978 for LBP purposes.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Delivery condition / require revised environmental report
business_rules.extraction_notes: Explicit dates and definitions (1978 cutoff; COO before 01/01/1978).

examples: Environmental site assessment report used in underwriting
Property condition assessment report required for delivery
Engineering report triggering repair escrow requirements

business_rules.rule_name: LBP sampling minimums vary at 100 units and 1.0 mg/cm2
business_rules.description: If LBP testing is performed, the environmental consultant must test a representative sampling of painted surfaces in units and common areas and may use XRF technology indicating lead exceeding 1.0 mg/cm2 or approved lab analysis of scraping samples. Minimum sample counts depend on property size: (1) For 100 or fewer units: 10 common area samples and unit samples from the greater of 10% of living units or 5 living units; in each living unit sampled, at least 3 LBP samples. (2) For more than 100 units: 10 common area samples and unit samples from the greater of 5% of living units or 10 living units; in each living unit sampled, at least 3 LBP samples.
business_rules.conditions: When an environmental consultant conducts LBP testing for a pre-1978 Property
business_rules.consequences: If met: LBP testing meets minimum standard; if violated: retest/revised report
business_rules.exceptions: None stated for minimum sample counts when testing is conducted

source_reference.section_id: Guide Chapter 61 – Pages 20-21 (LBP protocol and minimum samples)
source_reference.source_text: • Use of X -ray fluoroscope (XRF) technology (which indicates a presence of lead exceeding 1.0 mg/cm2) The environmental consultant must test a representative sampling of painted surfaces of both the units and the common areas. The required minimum number of samples depends on the size of the Property: • For a Property with 100 or fewer units, the environmental consultant must take the following samples: o Ten common area samples o Samples from the greater of 10 percent of the living units or five living units (In each living unit sampled, the environmental consultant must take at least three LBP samples.) • For a Property with more than 100 units, the environmental consultant must take the following samples: o Ten common area samples o Samples from the greater of 5 percent of the living units or 10 living units

examples: 80-unit property: 10 common-area samples + units from greater of 10% (8 units) or 5 units → sample 8 units, 3 samples each (24 unit samples).
150-unit property: 10 common-area samples + units from greater of 5% (7.5→8 units) or 10 units → sample 10 units, 3 samples each (30 unit samples).
Consultant tests only 2 samples per unit: does not meet minimum 3 per unit; Freddie requires supplemental testing.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Delivery condition / require additional sampling and updated findings
business_rules.extraction_notes: Multiple explicit numeric thresholds (100 units, 10%, 5%, 5/10 units, 3 per unit, 1.0 mg/cm2).

business_rules.rule_name: Non-municipal Water Tests Must Be ≤180 Days Old
business_rules.description: If the Property is not serviced by a municipal water system, the environmental consultant must determine compliance with applicable federal, State, and local requirements, including reviewing test results that are no more than 180 days old at the time of the site visit. If compliance cannot be determined, the consultant must obtain and analyze drinking water samples including a first draw sample and compare to State standards (or Federal Safe Drinking Water Act standards if no State program exists).
business_rules.conditions: Environmental assessment where Property is not served by municipal water
business_rules.consequences: If met, drinking water compliance is supported; if violated, additional testing is required before clearance
business_rules.exceptions: Municipal water system: testing is not required

source_reference.section_id: Chapter 61 – Environmental Requirements; Environmental assessment protocol (06/27/19)
source_reference.source_text: If the Property is not serviced by a municipal water system, the environmental consultant must determine if the drinking water is in compliance with applicable federal, State, and local requirements, which will include reviewing test results that are no mo re than 180 days old at the time of the site visit.   If the consultant cannot determine the compliance of the water system, then the environmental consultant must obtain and analyze drinking water samples for the Property that include a first draw sample.  The environmental consultant must test and compare these samples to State drinking water quality standards to determine whether compliance is currently achieved.  If no State program exists, Federal Safe Drinking Water Act standards apply.

examples: A rural property on a private well provides lab tests dated 90 days before the site visit: consultant may use them to assess compliance.
Well-water tests are 220 days old at site visit: consultant must obtain new samples including a first draw sample.
A property in a state without its own drinking water program: consultant compares results to Federal Safe Drinking Water Act standards.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Underwriting condition / Freddie may require additional testing before purchase

Property condition assessment report required for delivery

business_rules.rule_name: SBL Physical Risk Report must inspect 10% units and at least 3 total
business_rules.description: For an SBL Physical Risk Report interior review, the consultant must inspect sufficient units to accurately evaluate each building and its systems and may not inspect fewer than three units. The consultant must ensure all buildings and unit types are reviewed and must inspect: (i) 100% of all commercial units; and (ii) at least 10% of all residential units, including at least 50% of vacant units and at least 50% of Down Units. If Down Unit conditions vary considerably, additional units must be inspected to adequately identify rent-ready costs.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to SBL Physical Risk Report interior inspections of dwelling/commercial units
business_rules.consequences: If inspection sampling meets thresholds, report satisfies minimum scope; if not, report is deficient and must be corrected/replaced before reliance
business_rules.exceptions: Must inspect additional Down Units if conditions vary considerably

source_reference.section_id: Chapter 62SBL, Section 62SBL – b. Dwelling units, commercial units and building interior (06/30/16)
source_reference.source_text: The consultant must inspect sufficient units to accurately evaluate the condition of each building and its systems and in no case may inspect fewer than three units. In addition, the consultant must select the units to be inspected and ensure that all buildings and unit types are reviewed and that all of the following are inspected: • 100 percent of all commercial units • At least 10 percent of all residential units, to include: o At least 50 percent of vacant units, and o At least 50 percent of Down Units

examples: Property has 40 residential units and 2 commercial units: consultant must inspect both commercial units and at least 4 residential units, but also at least 3 total units minimum applies.

Property has 20 residential units with 6 vacant units: at least 2 of the inspected residential units must be vacant (≥50% of vacant units within the inspected set), while still meeting ≥10% residential overall.
Property has 30 residential units with 4 Down Units whose conditions differ widely: consultant must inspect additional Down Units beyond the 50% minimum to estimate rent-ready costs accurately.
business_rules.extraction_notes: Multiple explicit numeric sampling thresholds (3 units, 100%, 10%, 50%).

examples: Environmental site assessment report used in underwriting
Property condition assessment report required for delivery
Engineering report triggering repair escrow requirements

business_rules.rule_name: Electrical service minimum 60 amps or 40 amps with five conditions
business_rules.description: In the SBL Physical Risk Report inspection, the minimum acceptable electrical service per unit is 60 amperes. Electrical service of 40 amperes is the minimum allowable without a load analysis only if all of the following are met: no washers/dryers in units; no electric heating, stovetops/stovetop appliances, or water heaters; no A/C unless on a separate dedicated circuit; no atypical high-demand equipment (e.g., whirlpool tubs/freezers); and no units over 750 square feet. If service levels below 60 or 40 (as applicable) are encountered or reported, the consultant must perform or review a load analysis based on current NEC guidelines and make corrective recommendations. If inadequate power supply is found, Borrower must correct before Origination Date or list as a Priority Repair; property must meet NEC and all local building codes regardless.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when consultant evaluates electrical capacity in SBL Physical Risk Report
business_rules.consequences: If service meets 60 amps (or 40 with conditions), no load analysis required; if below minimum, load analysis and corrective actions/priority repairs are required
business_rules.exceptions: 40 amps allowed only when all five conditions are satisfied

source_reference.section_id: Chapter 62SBL – c. Problematic materials, equipment and systems, Electrical capacity (12/12/24)
source_reference.source_text: The minimum amperage acceptable to each unit is 60 amperes, except that 40 amperes is the minimum allowable service level acceptable without a load analysis, provided all of the following conditions are met: o No washing machines or clothes dryers are located in the units o No heating systems, stovetops, stovetop appliances, and water heaters are powered by electricity o No air conditioning units of any type are used unless serviced by a separate dedicated circuit o No atypical equipment with high electricity demands ... o No units are over 750 square feet If service levels less than the minimum allowable service levels (60 or 40 amperes, as applicable) are encountered ... the consultant must: • Perform a load analysis

examples: Units have 60-amp service: consultant documents compliance; no load analysis is required solely due to amperage.
Units have 40-amp service, are 700 sq ft, no in-unit laundry, no electric heat/stove/water heater, and A/C on dedicated circuit: 40 amps is acceptable without load analysis.
Units have 40-amp service but include electric water heaters: conditions for 40-amp exception fail; consultant must perform/review NEC-based load analysis and recommend corrective measures.
business_rules.extraction_notes: Clear numeric thresholds (60/40 amps; 750 sq ft) and explicit conditional exception plus required actions if below minimum.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Purchase eligibility condition / priority repair requirement

business_rules.rule_name: Property condition report must be dated within 6 months
business_rules.description: A property condition report (Form 1105, Property Condition Assessment) must be dated within six months prior to the date on which the full underwriting package is delivered to Freddie Mac. If this dating requirement is not met, the report is stale for initial underwriting submission and may require update/replacement before acceptance.
business_rules.conditions: Full underwriting package is being delivered to Freddie Mac with a property condition report included.
business_rules.consequences: If met, report satisfies recency requirement; if violated, Freddie Mac may require an updated report and delay underwriting review.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated for initial submission recency.

source_reference.source_text: c. Specific requirements for property condition reports ( 02/17/22) Form 1105, Property Condition Assessment,  must be completed and submitted as described in Section 62.6.  The property condition report must:  • Clearly state any limiting conditions and the intended purpose of the report  • Identify deviations from the Guide requirements and the most recent version of ASTM standard E2018  • Be dated within six months prior to the date on which the full underwriting package is delivered to Freddie Mac

examples: Underwriting package delivered to Freddie Mac on 2025-03-01; property condition report dated 2024-10-15 (under 6 months) is acceptable.
Underwriting package delivered on 2025-03-01; report dated 2024-07-15 (over 6 months) is stale and must be updated before Freddie Mac proceeds.
Report includes purpose and limiting conditions but is dated 7 months prior to delivery; it fails recency even if otherwise complete.

business_rules.rule_name: PCA site inspection must be within 30 days of report
business_rules.description: At initial submission of the full underwriting package to Freddie Mac, the site inspection supporting the property condition report must be dated within 30 days prior to the date of the property condition report. If the inspection is older than 30 days relative to the report date, the submission fails this timing requirement.
business_rules.conditions: Initial submission of full underwriting package to Freddie Mac includes a property condition report with a supporting site inspection.
business_rules.consequences: If met, site inspection recency is compliant; if violated, Freddie Mac may require a new inspection and/or updated report, delaying review.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated.

source_reference.source_text: At initial submission of the full underwriting package to Freddie Mac, the site inspection supporting the property condition report must be dated within 30 days prior to the date of the property condition report.

examples: Report date is 2025-02-01 and site inspection date is 2025-01-15; inspection is within 30 days and compliant.
Report date is 2025-02-01 and inspection date is 2024-12-15; inspection is 48 days prior and noncompliant—new inspection needed.
Inspection date is missing in the report; submission cannot evidence the 30-day requirement and is treated as deficient.

business_rules.rule_name: Routine repair item threshold is $3000 per item
business_rules.description: For property condition reporting, “Routine Repairs and Maintenance” include minor deficiencies or deferred maintenance items with a cost of $3000 or less per repair item. These routine items are not considered Critical Repairs or Priority Repairs and are expected to be completed in normal operations at nominal cost.
business_rules.conditions: Classifying repair and maintenance items in a property condition report for a multifamily property.
business_rules.consequences: If cost per item is ≤ $3000, classify as routine (not critical/priority); if > $3000 or otherwise severe, classify per applicable repair categories (e.g., priority/critical) and include in report.
business_rules.exceptions: Consultant may categorize as Priority Repair if lack of maintenance warrants.

source_reference.section_id: Guide Chapter 62 – Routine Repairs and Maintenance (12/12/24)
source_reference.source_text: • Routine Repairs and Maintenance – Repairs and maintenance that are expected to be completed by the Borrower in the normal course of property operations  and are nominal in cost.  These repairs are not considered to be Critical  Repairs  or Priority Repairs and include work that is:  o Often preventative in nature  o Accomplished within the Property’s normal operating budget  o Typically completed by on -site staff  o Focused on keeping the Property fully functioning and serviceable  o Minor Deficiencies or Deferred Maintenance  with a cost of $3000 or less per repair item

examples: A unit door closer replacement costing $650 is treated as routine repair and not a priority/critical repair.
A parking lot patch repair quoted at $2,950 is within the $3,000 threshold and is classified as routine maintenance.
A roof repair quoted at $12,000 is not routine by this definition and must be evaluated for priority/critical categorization.

business_rules.rule_name: Property occupancy must be at least 90% for 3 consecutive months before closing and as of delivery date
business_rules.description: For the three consecutive months prior to loan closing and as of the Delivery Date, at least 90% of the living units (or a higher level if required by the applicable purchase program/product to cover debt service and other required expenses) must have been occupied at rent levels that support the Freddie Mac Underwriting Value of the Property. Failure to satisfy this occupancy condition may render the Mortgage ineligible for delivery/purchase until cured.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to multifamily properties during underwriting prior to closing and delivery
business_rules.consequences: If occupancy meets threshold, property satisfies occupancy condition; if below threshold, mortgage may be ineligible for purchase/delivery until cured
business_rules.exceptions: Higher than 90% may be required by product/purchase program to cover expenses

source_reference.source_text: 8.4 Occupancy (02/07/05) For the three consecutive months prior to loan closing and as of the Delivery Date, at least 90 percent of the living units (or such higher level as may be necessary to cover debt service and pay all other expenses at the level required by the applicable purchase program or product) must have been occupied at rent levels that support the Freddie Mac Underwriting Value of the Property.

8.4 Occupancy (02/07/05) For the three consecutive months prior to loan closing and as of the Delivery Date, at least 90 percent of the living units (or such higher level as may be necessary to cover debt service and pay all other expenses at the level required by the applicable purchase program or product) must have been occupied at rent levels that support the Freddie Mac Underwriting Value of the Property.

examples: If occupancy is 89% in any of the three months prior to closing, the condition is not met.
If a program requires higher occupancy than 90% to cover required expenses, the higher program threshold governs.
business_rules.extraction_notes: Explicit 90% and three-month temporal requirement; includes product-driven higher-threshold clause.

examples: 200-unit garden-style apartment community in Texas
Mixed-use property with residential units above retail space
Affordable housing property with regulatory restrictions

deduplication_info.rationale: Both rules impose the same Minimum Occupancy requirement: for the three consecutive months prior to loan closing and as of the Delivery Date, at least 90% of living units must be occupied at rent levels supporting Freddie Mac Underwriting Value (or higher if required by the applicable program). Same timeframe, same 90% threshold, same delivery/closing context, same consequence (potential ineligibility). Wording differences are purely stylistic and rule_type labeling (constraint vs eligibility) does not change the business logic.
deduplication_info.primary_selection_reason: BR_MULTIFAMILY_PROPERTY_CONSTRAINT_005_009 selected as primary: equal completeness, but retained per priority 5 (earlier rule ID) with no loss of detail.

business_rules.rule_name: MHC resident rent increase requires 30 days written notice
business_rules.description: For Manufactured Housing Community (MHC) tenant protections, each Applicable MHC Resident must receive at least 30 days’ prior written notice of any rent increase.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to MHC Tenant Protections Document(s) for MHC Mortgages
business_rules.consequences: If not met, tenant protections terms fail Guide requirements and may block purchase
business_rules.exceptions: If requirement violates applicable law, it is void per Guide language

source_reference.section_id: Guide Chapter 22 – Originating a Manufactured Housing Community Mortgage; Tenant Protections item 2
source_reference.source_text: 2. Applicable MHC Resident must receive at least 30 -days’ prior written notice of any increase in rent.

examples: Lease amendment increases rent effective July 1; written notice must be delivered no later than June 1.
Property manager emails notice 20 days before increase; this violates the 30-day minimum and must be corrected before delivery.
State law requires 45 days’ notice; property must follow 45 days (more favorable law) and the 30-day term is not controlling.
business_rules.extraction_notes: Direct stated timeframe (30 days) for rent increase notice to MHC residents.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Freddie Mac purchase ineligibility until cured

examples: 200-unit garden-style apartment community in Texas
Mixed-use property with residential units above retail space
Affordable housing property with regulatory restrictions

business_rules.rule_name: MHC rent late grace 5 days and cure 10 days if none
business_rules.description: For MHC tenant protections, an Applicable MHC Resident must have a 5-day grace period for late rent. The resident must have the right to cure a rent-payment default within the lease’s cure period (if any). If the lease has no cure period for rent-payment default, the resident must have the right to cure within 10 days after expiration of the 5-day grace period.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to MHC pad site lease terms for Applicable MHC Residents
business_rules.consequences: If not met, tenant protections are noncompliant and may prevent Freddie Mac purchase
business_rules.exceptions: If requirement violates applicable law, it is void per Guide language

source_reference.section_id: Guide Chapter 22 – Originating a Manufactured Housing Community Mortgage; Tenant Protections item 3
source_reference.source_text: 3. Applicable MHC Resident is entitled to a 5 -day grace period for the failure to timely pay rent and has the right to cure any default in the payment of rent within the cure period set forth in its lease, if any.   If no cure period for a default in the payment of rent exists in its lease, then Applicable MHC Resident has the right to cure any default in the payment of rent within 10 days after the expiration of the 5 -day grace period described above.

examples: Rent due on the 1st; lease provides a 7-day cure period. Resident must still receive at least a 5-day grace period, and can cure within the lease cure period.
Lease is silent on cure; resident must be allowed to cure through day 15 (5-day grace + 10 additional days).
Lease provides only a 2-day grace period; this violates the required 5-day minimum and must be amended.
business_rules.extraction_notes: Two explicit timeframes (5-day grace; 10-day cure after grace when no cure period exists).
business_rules.enforcement_action: Freddie Mac purchase ineligibility until cured

business_rules.rule_name: Zoning and Building Code Conformity Required
business_rules.description: For a Mortgage to be eligible for purchase by Freddie Mac, the Property securing it must conform to all applicable zoning, subdivision and use laws, ordinances or codes and local building and housing codes.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to any Property securing a Mortgage delivered for purchase
business_rules.consequences: If conforming, property is eligible; if not conforming, loan may be ineligible
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in this subsection

examples: Property has a current certificate of occupancy and no zoning/building violations on record; meets conformity requirement.

business_rules.rule_name: Zoning Report Must Address 3 Determinations
business_rules.description: The Seller must submit a zoning report (per underwriting checklist) that is satisfactory to Freddie Mac and includes clear determinations regarding: (i) whether required Certificates of Occupancy have been issued and are in effect (or a statement per Section 8.5(e) if not available), (ii) whether the municipality/jurisdiction has any notice of zoning/building code violations on record, and (iii) whether the municipality/jurisdiction has any fire/life safety or accessibility violations on record (if not fully addressed in the Chapter 62 property condition report). If a zoning report is not available for the jurisdiction, Freddie Mac may purchase if the Property meets Section 8.5(d).
business_rules.conditions: Applies when underwriting checklist requires zoning report for the Property
business_rules.consequences: If complete/satisfactory, supports eligibility; if missing, delivery may be conditioned/denied
business_rules.exceptions: If zoning report not available, Freddie Mac may purchase per 8.5(d)

source_reference.source_text: b. The Seller must submit a zoning report as specified on the applicable underwriting checklist. The zoning report must be satisfactory to Freddie Mac, and must include clear determinations with respect to the following: • Whether all Certificates of Occupancy required for the use, operation and occupancy of the Property for its presently-contemplated use have been issued and are in effect or, if all Certificates of Occupancy are not available, a statement in accordance with Section 8.5(e), • Whether the applicable municipality or other jurisdiction has on record any notice of violations of applicable zoning laws and ordinances and building codes, and • If not fully addressed in the property condition report delivered pursuant to Chapter 62, whether the applicable municipality or other jurisdiction has on record any notice of violations relating to fire and life safety or accessibility requirements applicable to the Property If a zoning report is not available for the jurisdiction in which the Property is located, Freddie Mac may purchase a Mortgage if the Property meets the requirements of Section 8.5(d) below.

examples: Zoning report confirms all certificates of occupancy are issued/in effect, and no violations are on record; requirement satisfied.
Zoning report omits the fire/life safety and accessibility violation determination and Chapter 62 report does not cover it; report is incomplete and must be corrected.
Jurisdiction does not provide zoning reports; loan may proceed only if the file documents how Section 8.5(d) requirements are met.

Affordable housing property with regulatory restrictions

business_rules.rule_name: Earthquake SEL-475 Thresholds 20% and 40% Determine Insurance or Retrofit
business_rules.description: For a Property in an Elevated Seismic Hazard Region, Freddie Mac requires a Seismic Risk Assessment (SRA) at the Borrower’s expense. SEL-475 estimates must be provided for each building construction type when multiple types exist. Purchase eligibility and earthquake mitigation depend on SEL-475 and building stability concern: (i) SEL-475 ≤ 20% with stability concern = ineligible for purchase until seismic retrofit completed unless otherwise approved; (ii) SEL-475 > 20% and ≤ 40% = insurance required and retrofit optional, but stability concern still makes the building ineligible until retrofit unless otherwise approved; (iii) SEL-475 > 40% = seismic retrofit required prior to submission to Freddie Mac for consideration. Where earthquake insurance is required, coverage must be at least 100% of the building’s Insurable Value (IV).
business_rules.conditions: Applies to Properties in Elevated Seismic Hazard Region under Chapter 31.9
business_rules.consequences: If thresholds met, require insurance/retrofit; if stability concern, property is ineligible until retrofit unless approved
business_rules.exceptions: Ineligibility may be overridden if otherwise approved by Freddie Mac

source_reference.source_text: Freddie Mac requires an SRA at the Borrower’s expense for a Property located in an Elevated Seismic Hazard Region... a SEL-475 estimate is required for each building construction type. If any single building has a SEL-475 greater than 20 percent, then earthquake insurance or seismic retrofit is required for that building... SEL-475... ≤ 20%... Ineligible for purchase until seismic retrofit completed unless otherwise approved by Freddie Mac... > 20% & ≤ 40% Insurance required... Ineligible for purchase until seismic retrofit completed unless otherwise approved... > 40% The affected building(s) must have a seismic retrofit prior to the Mortgage being submitted to Freddie Mac for consideration... coverage must be at least 100 percent of the IV of the building

examples: Property has SEL-475 of 18% but building stability concern is identified; property is ineligible for purchase until seismic retrofit is completed unless Freddie Mac approves otherwise.
Property has two building types; tuck-under parking buildings have SEL-475 25% and garden-style buildings 15%; earthquake insurance or retrofit is required for the 25% building type.
Property has SEL-475 45%; Seller must ensure seismic retrofit is completed before submitting the Mortgage to Freddie Mac for consideration.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Ineligible for purchase until seismic retrofit completed

examples: 200-unit garden-style apartment community in Texas
Mixed-use property with residential units above retail space

business_rules.rule_name: Reserve required when priority repairs total at least $25,000
business_rules.description: When conditions at the Property become Priority Repairs, Freddie Mac requires a Reserve for all identified Priority Repairs (including PR-90 Repairs) if the estimated total cost to complete all such Priority Repairs identified in the SBL Physical Risk Report is $25,000 or greater.
business_rules.conditions: Applies after SBL Physical Risk Report identifies Priority Repairs at property
business_rules.consequences: Met: reserve required/established; Violated: commitment/purchase conditions unmet
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in excerpt

source_reference.section_id: 8SBL.17 SBL Physical Risk Report, reserve trigger
source_reference.source_text: 4. If any of the following conditions exist at the Property and become Priority Repairs, Freddie Mac will require a Reserve for all identified Priority Repairs (including PR -90 Repairs): • The estimated cost to complete all Priority Repairs (including PR -90 Repairs) identified in the SBL Physical Risk Report is $25,000 or greater, or

examples: Physical risk report identifies roof and electrical repairs totaling $30,000; a reserve for all identified Priority Repairs is required.
Report identifies Priority Repairs totaling $24,999; this $25,000 trigger is not met (other triggers may still apply if stated elsewhere).
Report identifies PR-90 Repairs totaling $25,000 exactly; reserve requirement applies because cost is “$25,000 or greater.”
200-unit garden-style apartment community in Texas
Mixed-use property with residential units above retail space
Affordable housing property with regulatory restrictions

business_rules.rule_name: Seniors housing ineligible if skilled nursing NOI exceeds 20%
business_rules.description: A Mortgage secured by a Seniors Housing Property is not eligible for purchase by Freddie Mac if (1) more than 20% of the Net Operating Income is derived from skilled nursing care, acute medical care, or rehabilitative care services, or (2) the Property has a skilled nursing component that does not allocate a minimum of 15% of the total skilled nursing units for assisted living or independent living residents who were residents prior to entering the skilled nursing facility.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to Seniors Housing Properties during mortgage eligibility review
business_rules.consequences: Meets thresholds: may be eligible; violates: mortgage not eligible for purchase
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.source_text: b. Ineligible Properties (05/11/10)  If a Mortgage is secured by a Property with either of the following conditions, the Mortgage is not eligible for purchase by Freddie Mac:  • A Property in which more than 20 percent of the Net Operating Income is derived from the provision of skilled nursing care, acute medical care or rehabilitative care services, or  • A Property that has a skilled nursing component that does not allocate a minimum of 15 percent of the total number of skilled nursing units for the assisted living or independent living residents of the Property

examples: Property derives 25% of NOI from skilled nursing services: ineligible for purchase.
Property derives 18% of NOI from skilled nursing, but allocates only 10% of skilled nursing units for existing AL/IL residents: ineligible.
Property derives 10% of NOI from skilled nursing and allocates 20% of skilled nursing units for existing residents: passes this eligibility screen.

Mixed-use property with residential units above retail space

business_rules.rule_name: Property Must Have 5+ Units and Completed Construction
business_rules.description: To be eligible, the Property must (i) contain five or more dwelling units, (ii) be designed in whole or in part for residential use, (iii) have completed construction (including subsurface utilities and off-site improvements) as of the Delivery Date, and (iv) have each residential unit include kitchen and bathroom facilities. The Property must be served by public water and sanitary sewer systems. If improvements cannot be completed for valid reasons (e.g., inclement weather), an adequate Reserve for incomplete items must be established, and a satisfactory completion certificate with color photographs must be submitted to Freddie Mac.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to Properties securing Mortgages delivered to Freddie Mac
business_rules.consequences: If met, Property may be acceptable; if not, delivery/approval may be rejected
business_rules.exceptions: Incomplete improvements allowed only with adequate Reserve and completion certificate

source_reference.section_id: Chapter 8, Section 8.2(a) Structure type and habitability (06/29/18)
source_reference.source_text: The Property must contain five or more dwelling units and must be designed, in whole or in part, for residential use. Construction of the Property, including subsurface utilities and off-site improvements, must be completed as of the Delivery Date. If improvements cannot be completed for valid reasons, such as inclement weather, an adequate Reserve for the incomplete items must be established. Reserves must also be established for any repairs, improvements, alterations, conditions or construction required by the appraiser, engineer and/or Freddie Mac. A satisfactory completion certificate, accompanied by color photographs, must be submitted to Freddie Mac. Each residential unit in the Property must contain kitchen and bathroom facilities. The Property must be served by public water and sanitary sewer systems.

examples: A 120-unit apartment property with completed utilities and a completion certificate meets the 5+ unit and construction-complete requirements.
A 40-unit property with incomplete parking-lot resurfacing may proceed only if an adequate Reserve is established and a completion certificate with color photos is submitted.
A 10-unit property on private well/septic fails the public water and sanitary sewer requirement and may be deemed ineligible.
200-unit garden-style apartment community in Texas
Mixed-use property with residential units above retail space
Affordable housing property with regulatory restrictions

business_rules.rule_name: Borrower Prohibited From Home Sharing Rentals Under 1 Month
business_rules.description: The Borrower must not participate in home sharing activities, defined as short-term rentals (typically less than one month) marketed through a peer-to-peer online marketplace or home sharing platform. The Borrower must also not enter into leases (including master leases) of residential, corporate, or commercial units that the Borrower knows or should have known are intended to be used by tenants for full or part-time home sharing activities. Examples of platforms include Airbnb, VRBO, and booking.com.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to Properties securing Freddie Mac Mortgages under Chapter 8
business_rules.consequences: If violated, the Property/Mortgage may be noncompliant and subject to rejection/servicing action
business_rules.exceptions: None

source_reference.section_id: Chapter 8, Section 8.2(a) Structure type and habitability (06/29/18)
source_reference.source_text: The Borrower must not participate in home sharing activities, which are defined as short -term rentals (typically less than one month) that are marketed through a peer -to-peer online marketplace or a home sharing platform, nor enter into leases, including master leases, of residential, corporate or commercial units that the Borrower knows or should have known are intended to be used by the tenants for full or part -time home sharing activities. For clarity, nonexclusive examples of home sharing platforms include Airbnb, VRBO, and booking.com.

examples: A borrower allowing 2-week rentals advertised on Airbnb violates the home sharing prohibition.
A borrower signing a master lease to a corporate tenant known to sublet units on VRBO violates the prohibition.
A borrower operating only 12-month leases with no short-term marketing on peer-to-peer platforms complies.

business_rules.rule_name: Wood-Damaging Insect Report Dated Within 6 Months
business_rules.description: If the Property has wood framing or structural members, the file must include either (i) a wood-damaging insect inspection report stating there is no evidence of infestation, or (ii) a certification from the Property’s current pest control provider stating there is no evidence of infestation and that the Property is regularly inspected and/or treated. The report/certification must be dated within six months prior to submission of the full underwriting package to Freddie Mac and comply with applicable federal, State, and local regulations. Freddie Mac will require a Repair Reserve for costs to repair any areas damaged by wood-damaging insects. Documentation is not required if all three conditions are met: contract in place, contract remains for term of Mortgage, and no evidence of wood damage per the Property Condition Report (if applicable).
business_rules.conditions: Applies when Property has wood framing/structural members per PCR/PRR
business_rules.consequences: If missing/outdated, underwriting may be suspended; damage requires Repair Reserve
business_rules.exceptions: No doc required if contract in place + remains term + no wood damage per PCR

source_reference.section_id: Chapter 8, Section 8.2(e) Wood-damaging insect inspection reports (12/17/19)
source_reference.source_text: For any Property with wood framing or structural members as described above, the following documentation must be provided: • A wood -damaging insect inspection report stating that there is no evidence of wood -damaging insect infestation, or • Certification from the Property’s current pest control provider stating that there is no evidence of wood -damaging insect infestation and that the Property is regularly inspected and/or treated to prevent wood -damaging insect infestation . The wood -damaging insect inspection report or the certification from the Property’s current pest control provider must be dated within six months prior to the date of the submission of the full underwriting package to Freddie Mac and must be in compliance with all applicable federal, State and local regulations. Freddie Mac will require a Repair Reserve for the costs to repair any areas damaged by wood -damaging insects. Notwithstanding the above, the documentation listed above is not required if the following three conditions are satisfied: • The Borrower provides documentation confirming that there is a wood -damaging insect contract in place for the Property , • A wood -damaging insect contract(s) will remain in place for the term of the Mortgage, and • There is no evidence of wood damage per the Property Condition Report (if applicable) .

examples: A wood-framed property submits a termite inspection report dated 4 months before underwriting package submission; requirement is satisfied.
A wood-framed property submits a pest provider certification dated 9 months before submission; requirement fails due to the 6-month dating limit.
A property has a documented pest contract that will remain for the full term and the PCR shows no wood damage; inspection report may be omitted.

business_rules.rule_name: SBL mixed-use limits: commercial income ≤ 40% and space ≤ 40%
business_rules.description: For SBL Mortgages on mixed-use properties, purchase eligibility requires that underwritten commercial income is no more than 40% of Gross Potential Rent (GPR) and square footage devoted to commercial space is no more than 40% of the Net Leasable Space; other listed mixed-use conditions (zoning compatibility, market support) must also be satisfied.
business_rules.conditions: When SBL collateral includes any commercial (nonresidential) space or income
business_rules.consequences: Meets: SBL mixed-use eligibility satisfied; Violated: Mortgage ineligible for SBL purchase
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.source_text: Mortgages on such Properties are eligible for purchase under Freddie Mac's SBL Purchase Product, provided that each of the following conditions is satisfied: ... Underwritten commercial income is no more than 40 percent of Gross Potential Rent (GPR) and is otherwise acceptable to Freddie Mac ... Square footage devoted to commercial space is no more than 40 percent of the Net Leasable Space and is otherwise acceptable to Freddie Mac

examples: GPR $1,000,000; underwritten commercial income $350,000 (35%) and commercial NLS 25%; eligible on these thresholds.
GPR $800,000; underwritten commercial income $360,000 (45%); fails commercial income limit and is ineligible.
Commercial NLS 42% even with commercial income 30%; fails space limit and is ineligible.

business_rules.rule_name: MHC allows under 60 amps only if code compliant
business_rules.description: A Manufactured Housing Community (MHC) with electrical power under 60 amperes is permitted only if the property condition consultant confirms electrical power meets the minimum requirements of all State and local building codes. If electrical power is below any State or local building code requirements, the property condition consultant must confirm the current power level is sufficient for the MHC (no load calculation required) and recommend corrective measures per Section 22.6(c) or Section 62.5(c), as applicable.
business_rules.conditions: When an MHC property has electrical service under 60 amperes
business_rules.consequences: If met: acceptable for underwriting; if violated: corrective measures required
business_rules.exceptions: Permitted under 60 amps only with code-compliance confirmation

source_reference.source_text: A Manufactured Housing Community with electrical power under 60 amperes is permitted, provided that the property condition consultant confirms electrical power meets the minimum requirement of all State and local building codes. If the electrical power is below the requirements of any State or local building codes, the property condition consultant must: • Confirm the current power level is sufficient for the Manufactured Housing Community (although no load calculation is necessary) • Recommend corrective measures as outlined in Section 22.6(c) or Section 62.5(c), as applicable

examples: MHC has 50-amp service but local code minimum is 50 amps: consultant confirms compliance; property permitted.
MHC has 50-amp service but local code minimum is 60 amps: consultant confirms sufficiency and recommends upgrades.
Consultant does not address code minimums: Freddie requires revised report or repair plan before approval.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Delivery condition / require repair plan or upgrades
business_rules.extraction_notes: Clear numeric threshold (60 amperes) and conditional requirements.

examples: 200-unit garden-style apartment community in Texas
Mixed-use property with residential units above retail space
Affordable housing property with regulatory restrictions

business_rules.rule_name: Elevated Seismic Regions Require Level 1 SRA If Any Factor Exists
business_rules.description: If the Property is located in an Elevated Seismic Hazard Region (excluding MHC Property), the Seller/Servicer must evaluate the Property for listed seismic risk factors (including: building within 50 feet of a mapped earthquake fault trace or within an Alquist-Priolo Earthquake Fault Zone in California; high-rise buildings 8 stories or greater; reinforced concrete or CMU bearing wall buildings constructed prior to 2000; wood-framed buildings constructed prior to 1960; and other listed factors). If none of the listed factors are present, a Level 0 SRA is required. If any factor is present—or if the Seller/Servicer cannot conclusively determine none are present—a Level 1 SRA is required.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when Property is in an Elevated Seismic Hazard Region (non-MHC)
business_rules.consequences: If correct SRA level obtained, seismic risk assessment is compliant; if not, underwriting package is deficient
business_rules.exceptions: Does not apply to Mortgage secured by an MHC Property

source_reference.source_text: If the Property is located in an Elevated Seismic Hazard Region, the Seller/Servicer must evaluate the Property for the following seismic risk factors: 1. A building located within 50 feet of a mapped earthquake fault trace or located within an Alquist -Priolo Earthquake Fault Zone

examples: Property is in an Elevated Seismic Hazard Region and includes an 11-story tower (≥8 stories); Seller/Servicer must require a Level 1 SRA.
Property is in an Elevated Seismic Hazard Region and documentation confirms none of the listed risk factors apply; Seller/Servicer obtains a Level 0 SRA.
Seller/Servicer cannot conclusively verify whether the site is within 50 feet of a mapped fault trace; Level 1 SRA is required due to inability to conclude 'none present'.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Delivery rejection or purchase delay pending SRA completion
business_rules.extraction_notes: Explicit decision logic for Level 0 vs Level 1 SRA plus multiple numeric triggers (50 feet, 8 stories, pre-2000, pre-1960).

examples: 200-unit garden-style apartment community in Texas
Mixed-use property with residential units above retail space
Affordable housing property with regulatory restrictions

business_rules.rule_name: Infrastructure Agreement Must Not Impose Obligations of $50,000 or More
business_rules.description: A lease created pursuant to an Infrastructure Agreement is acceptable for an SBL Property only if it contains market-customary terms and does not impose financial obligations equal to or greater than $50,000 on the property owner, does not impose any financial obligations on any mortgagee, and does not impose obligations on a subsequent owner following foreclosure, deed-in-lieu, or other conveyance (including reimbursement of an up-front fee upon termination).
business_rules.conditions: When an SBL Property is subject to (or will be subject to) an Infrastructure Agreement lease
business_rules.consequences: If conditions met, lease may be acceptable; if violated, lease terms must be modified or loan may be declined
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.source_text: A lease created pursuant to an Infrastructure Agreement (as defined in Section 8.22) is acceptable for an SBL Property , provided, in addition to the requirements specified in Section 8.22, that it (a) contains terms and provisions, including compensation, that are customary for the market in which the Property is located, and (b) does not impose any financial obligations equal to or greater than $50,000 on the Property owner, and does not impose any financial obligations on any mortgagee, and/or subsequent owner by foreclosure, deed-in-lieu of foreclosure or other conveyance (including reimbursement of any up-front fee pai d by the provider to the Property owner upon termination of the agreement).

examples: A cell tower lease requires the property owner to remove equipment and restore the roof at a capped cost of $40,000; it can be acceptable if no mortgagee obligations exist.
A broadband easement agreement obligates the owner to reimburse a $75,000 up-front fee if terminated; it is not acceptable because obligations are ≥ $50,000.
A solar provider agreement requires the lender/foreclosure purchaser to assume maintenance fees; it is not acceptable because it imposes obligations on a mortgagee/subsequent owner.
business_rules.extraction_notes: Clear acceptance condition with explicit $50,000 threshold and prohibition on mortgagee/successor obligations.

examples: 200-unit garden-style apartment community in Texas
Mixed-use property with residential units above retail space
Affordable housing property with regulatory restrictions

business_rules.rule_name: Title Policy Amount Must Be at Least Original Principal Balance
business_rules.description: The title insurance policy must insure the mortgagee for an amount that is no less than the original principal balance of the insured Mortgage. This establishes the minimum policy amount requirement for Freddie Mac delivery/acceptance purposes.
business_rules.conditions: When issuing title insurance for a Freddie Mac-insured Mortgage
business_rules.consequences: Policy amount sufficient: compliant; insufficient: delivery defect/condition
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.section_id: Chapter 29, Section e. Amount of protection (08/17/23)
source_reference.source_text: e. Amount of protection (0 8/17/23 ) The Title Policy must insure the mortgagee for an amount no less than the original principal balance of the insured Mortgage.

examples: Original principal balance is $12,500,000; title policy amount is $12,500,000: compliant.
Original principal balance is $7,000,000; title policy amount is $6,500,000: not compliant; must be increased to at least $7,000,000.
Original principal balance is $3,200,000; title policy amount is $4,000,000: compliant (exceeds minimum).
ALTA loan policy issued with required endorsements
Title policy with specific permitted easement exceptions
Title policy requiring curative action for a recorded lien

business_rules.rule_name: Flood deductible caps: $50k, $500k, 15 days, $100k
business_rules.description: Flood insurance policies must not exceed the maximum deductible limits stated in the Guide. For first-layer building coverage, the maximum deductible is $50,000 per building when the Property has 10 buildings or less in SFHAs, and $500,000 per occurrence when the Property has more than 10 buildings in SFHAs. For Business Income/Rental Value flood coverage, the maximum deductible is a 15-day waiting period (time-based) or $100,000 per occurrence (monetary). When NFIP policies are used as part of the coverage, the NFIP maximum deductible for the building type is acceptable.
business_rules.conditions: When placing or renewing flood insurance coverage for buildings located in SFHAs
business_rules.consequences: If met: policy is deductible-compliant; if violated: policy must be corrected/replaced to meet limits
business_rules.exceptions: NFIP maximum deductible for insured building type is acceptable when NFIP used

source_reference.source_text: The following are maximum deductibles allowed for flood insurance policies: For first -layer building coverage: • $50,000 per building for a Property with 10 buildings or less located in SFHAs • $500,000 per occurrence for a Property with more than 10 buildings located in SFHAs For Business Income/Rental Value coverage: • 15 day waiting period when expressed as a time -based deductible • $100,000 per occurrence when expressed as a monetary deductible When NFIP policies are used as part of the coverage, the maximum deductible available under the NFIP for the type of building being insured is acceptable.

examples: Property has 8 buildings in SFHA; building flood deductible is $75,000 per building → noncompliant (max $50,000) and must be reduced.
Property has 12 buildings in SFHA; policy uses $600,000 per occurrence deductible → noncompliant (max $500,000) and must be corrected.
Business income flood coverage uses a 30-day waiting period deductible → noncompliant (max 15 days) unless converted to an allowed deductible.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Insurance coverage cure requirement and potential force-place

examples: Property hazard policy with Freddie Mac named as mortgagee/loss payee
Insurance renewal evidence provided prior to policy expiration
Policy change increasing deductible beyond permitted limits

business_rules.rule_name: Earthquake Deductibles Capped at $50k, $100k, or 7.5% of TIV
business_rules.description: When earthquake insurance is required, the maximum allowable deductible depends on limit type and expression. For Specific Insurance Limits: if the Insurable Value (IV) is less than $10 million, maximum deductible is $50,000; if IV is at least $10 million, maximum deductible is $100,000. If expressed as a percentage, the deductible is 7.5% of the subject property’s Total Insurable Value (TIV), with a stated minimum not to exceed $100,000. For Blanket Insurance Limits: if expressed as a dollar amount, deductible is $250,000; if expressed as a percentage, deductible is 7.5% of TIV with a stated minimum not to exceed $250,000.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when Freddie Mac requires earthquake insurance under Chapter 31.9
business_rules.consequences: If deductible within caps, policy is compliant; if above caps, insurance is noncompliant
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.source_text: For Specific Insurance Limits: When expressed as a dollar amount: IV of the Property Maximum deductible < $10 million $50,000 ≥ $10 million $100,000 When expressed as a percentage, 7.5 percent of the subject property’s TIV, with a stated minimum not to exceed $100,000 For Blanket Insurance Limits: When expressed as a dollar amount, $250,000 When expressed as a percentage, 7.5 percent of the subject property’s TIV, with a stated minimum not to exceed $250,000

examples: Specific limit policy on a building with IV of $9,500,000 uses a $60,000 deductible: noncompliant because max is $50,000.
Specific limit policy with IV of $12,000,000 uses a $100,000 deductible: compliant (does not exceed $100,000 cap).
Blanket policy uses 7.5% of TIV with a stated minimum of $300,000: noncompliant because the stated minimum may not exceed $250,000.
Property hazard policy with Freddie Mac named as mortgagee/loss payee
Insurance renewal evidence provided prior to policy expiration
Policy change increasing deductible beyond permitted limits

business_rules.rule_name: Insurer must be AM Best A- and size VII or better
business_rules.description: Each insurance carrier providing property damage and/or liability insurance must maintain a minimum AM Best Company rating of A- financial strength and a financial size category of VII or better. Policies issued through State-Sponsored Insurance programs or insurers participating in the National Flood Insurance Program (NFIP) are exempt from these rating requirements.
business_rules.conditions: When a carrier provides property damage and/or liability insurance
business_rules.consequences: If met, carrier is acceptable; if not met, policy/carrier is unacceptable
business_rules.exceptions: Does not apply to State-Sponsored programs or NFIP insurers

source_reference.source_text: 31.4 Acceptable insurers (0 7/15/25) Each insurance carrier providing property damage and/or liability insurance, whether admitted or non-admitted, must maintain a minimum AM Best Company rating of A - financial strength and a financial size category of VII or better. These rating requirements do not apply to policies issued through State -Sponsored Insurance programs or policies issued by insurers participating in National Flood Insurance Program (NFIP).

examples: Carrier rated AM Best A (FSR) and size VIII: acceptable for All-Risk coverage.
Carrier rated AM Best B++ (FSR) size VII: unacceptable; policy must be replaced or re-marketed.
NFIP flood policy issued by an NFIP-participating insurer: AM Best rating test not required.
Property hazard policy with Freddie Mac named as mortgagee/loss payee
Insurance renewal evidence provided prior to policy expiration
Policy change increasing deductible beyond permitted limits

business_rules.rule_name: All-Risk coverage must be 100% IV single or 90% IV multi
business_rules.description: Property damage (All-Risk) insurance is required for all Mortgages and must be written on a replacement cost value basis rather than actual cash value basis (roof coverings may be actual cash value). Coverage amount must be not less than 100% of Insurable Value (IV) for properties with a single residential building and not less than 90% of IV for properties with multiple residential buildings. The policy must either not contain a Coinsurance Clause or must offset it with an Agreed Amount Provision where the agreed amount is no less than the estimated IV.
business_rules.conditions: When placing/renewing All-Risk property damage insurance on a Mortgage
business_rules.consequences: If met, coverage amount satisfies Freddie Mac minimums; if violated, coverage is deficient
business_rules.exceptions: Roof coverings may be written on an actual cash value basis

source_reference.source_text: 31.5 Property damage (All -Risk) insurance ( 12/16/25) Property damage insurance is required for all Mortgages ... Policies must be written on a replacement cost value basis rather than actual cash value basis; however, c overage for roof coverings may be written on an actual cash value basis. Property damage (All -Risk) insurance coverage must : • For properties with a single residential building, be written in an amount not less than 100 percent of the Insurable Value (IV) ... and • For properties with multiple residential buildings, be written in an amount not less than 90 percent of the IV, and • Either not contain a Coinsurance Clause or contain a Coinsurance Clause that is offset by an Agreed Amount Provision. If an Agreed Amount Provision is used, the agreed amount must be no less than the estimated IV.

examples: Single-building property with IV $12,000,000: coverage must be at least $12,000,000.
Campus with 4 residential buildings and IV $20,000,000: coverage must be at least $18,000,000 (90% of IV).
Policy has a coinsurance clause but includes agreed amount equal to estimated IV: acceptable offset.
Property hazard policy with Freddie Mac named as mortgagee/loss payee
Insurance renewal evidence provided prior to policy expiration
Policy change increasing deductible beyond permitted limits

business_rules.rule_name: All-Risk deductible caps are $50k or $100k; blanket max $250k
business_rules.description: For property damage (All-Risk) insurance policies providing Specific Insurance Limits, the maximum deductible per occurrence is $50,000 when the Property IV is < $10 million and $100,000 when the Property IV is ≥ $10 million. When All-Risk coverage is provided by a Blanket Insurance Limit, the maximum per occurrence deductible is $250,000.
business_rules.conditions: When evaluating All-Risk deductible for Specific Limits or Blanket Limits coverage
business_rules.consequences: If met, deductible is within cap; if exceeded, deductible is noncompliant
business_rules.exceptions: Expanded deductible waiver may apply for existing Mortgages (see related rule)

source_reference.source_text: a. Property damage (All -Risk) deductible ( 12/16/25) The maximum deductible per occurrence for property damage (All -Risk) insurance policies providing Specific Insurance Limits is: IV of the Property Maximum Deductible < $10 million $50,000 ≥ $10 million $100,000 The maximum per occurrence deductible when All -Risk coverage is provided by a Blanket Insurance Limit is $250,000.

examples: Property IV $9,500,000 on specific limits policy: deductible must be ≤ $50,000 per occurrence.
Property IV $10,000,000 on specific limits policy: deductible must be ≤ $100,000 per occurrence.
Blanket All-Risk policy: deductible $300,000 per occurrence is noncompliant because it exceeds $250,000.
Property hazard policy with Freddie Mac named as mortgagee/loss payee
Insurance renewal evidence provided prior to policy expiration
Policy change increasing deductible beyond permitted limits

business_rules.rule_name: Expanded deductible waiver requires 4x liquidity, risk rating ≤6, no 12-mo delinquency
business_rules.description: For existing Mortgages, if the Borrower cannot obtain deductibles that comply with the maximum deductibles in Chapter 31, the Servicer may approve expanded maximum deductibles for specific-limit policies (excluding NFIP, windstorm, and earthquake) only if all conditions are met: Borrower/Borrower Principal liquidity is at least four times the maximum deductible amount; Mortgage risk rating is six or less; Mortgage is not currently delinquent and has not been delinquent within the last 12 months; and Property condition is average or better per most recent inspection. Expanded maximum deductibles are capped at $100,000 (< $10 million IV) and $150,000 (≥ $10 million IV). Waiver is valid for one policy term and may be renewed if continued inability to obtain compliant deductibles.
business_rules.conditions: Existing Mortgage where Borrower cannot obtain Chapter 31 compliant deductibles
business_rules.consequences: If all conditions met, expanded deductible may be approved for 1 term; else must comply with standard caps
business_rules.exceptions: Not for NFIP, windstorm, or earthquake; waiver limited to one policy term

source_reference.source_text: valid for one policy term. At the end of that period, if the Borrower has been unable to obtain deductibles in compliance with the other applicable sections of this chapter, the Servicer may permit

examples: Existing loan, IV $8,000,000, requested deductible $90,000: allowed if liquidity ≥ $360,000, risk rating 6 or less, no delinquency in 12 months, and property condition average or better.
Existing loan, IV $12,000,000, requested deductible $175,000: not allowed because it exceeds $150,000 cap even if all other conditions are met.
Existing loan meets caps but had a 30-day delinquency 6 months ago: waiver not permitted due to delinquency within last 12 months.

Insurance renewal evidence provided prior to policy expiration
Policy change increasing deductible beyond permitted limits

business_rules.rule_name: Business income coverage must cover 12 months; deductible max 72 hours
business_rules.description: Business Income/Rental Value Insurance is required for all applicable property damage perils within the scope of the Special Causes of Loss/All Risk form. Coverage must be sufficient to cover either (i) Actual Loss Sustained for 12 months, (ii) 12 months of Effective Gross Income based on most recent annual EGI, or (iii) Annual NOI plus continuing expenses with a completed business income worksheet. Maximum deductible is the maximum deductible for the property insurance coverage or a waiting period of three days/72 hours. For a Mortgage Loan with UPB of $35 million or more, coverage must include a 90-day extended period.
business_rules.conditions: When Business Income/Rental Value coverage is required for All-Risk perils
business_rules.consequences: If met, BI/RV coverage meets Freddie Mac minimums; if violated, BI/RV coverage is insufficient
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in excerpt

source_reference.source_text: 31.6 Business Income/Rental Value Insurance (0 7/15/25) Business Income/Rental Value Insurance is required for all applicable property damage perils within the scope of the “ Special Causes of Loss” or “All Risk” policy form ... The coverage must be sufficient to cover the following: • Income based on Actual Loss Sustained for 12 months ; or • 12 months of Effective Gross Income (EGI) ...; or • Annual NOI plus continuing expenses ... The maximum deductible for business income/rental value coverage is: • The maximum deductible for the property insurance coverage, or • A waiting period ... of three days or 72 hours For a Mortgage Loan with a UPB of $35 million or more, coverage must include a 90 -day extended period

examples: BI coverage written as Actual Loss Sustained with 12-month period and 72-hour waiting period: compliant.
BI coverage limited to 6 months of EGI: noncompliant because it does not meet the 12-month minimum options.
Loan UPB $40,000,000 with BI coverage but no 90-day extended period: noncompliant for ≥$35MM UPB requirement.
Property hazard policy with Freddie Mac named as mortgagee/loss payee
Insurance renewal evidence provided prior to policy expiration
Policy change increasing deductible beyond permitted limits

business_rules.rule_name: Insurance Policy Term Minimum 12 Months and Continuous
business_rules.description: Insurance policies should be written for a term of at least 12 months. For a newly-originated Mortgage, a Property may be added mid-term to an existing 12-month policy. Policies of less than 12 months may be accepted only if, upon expiration, the policy will be renewed for at least 12 months. Policies must be in full force and effect on the Mortgage Origination Date and coverage must be continuous throughout the Mortgage term.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to property damage and liability policies for Freddie Mac Mortgages
business_rules.consequences: Meets rule: delivery/servicing insurance term is acceptable; violated: must correct/renew or loan may be ineligible
business_rules.exceptions: Policies <12 months allowed only if renewed for ≥12 months at expiry

source_reference.source_text: Insurance policies should be written for a term of at least 12 months. For a newly-originated Mortgage, a Property may be added mid-term to an existing 12-month policy. Policies of lesser terms may be accepted if, when the existing policy expires, the policy will be renewed for at least 12 months. Policies are to be in full force and effect on the Origination Date of the Mortgage and required coverage must be continuous throughout the term of the Mortgage.

examples: A 12-month property policy effective on the Origination Date satisfies the term requirement.
A property is added to an existing master policy with 8 months remaining; acceptable if renewal will be for at least 12 months at expiration.
A 6-month policy with no documented 12-month renewal commitment is not acceptable and must be replaced or extended.
business_rules.extraction_notes: Explicit minimum policy term and continuity requirements with renewal condition.

examples: Property hazard policy with Freddie Mac named as mortgagee/loss payee
Insurance renewal evidence provided prior to policy expiration
Policy change increasing deductible beyond permitted limits

business_rules.rule_name: Insurance Cancellation Notice 10 Days Nonpay and 30 Days Other
business_rules.description: Unless required otherwise by State law, each property damage insurance policy must require the insurer to notify the named mortgagee in writing at least 10 days before cancellation for nonpayment of premium or nonrenewal, and at least 30 days before cancellation for any other reason. General liability and umbrella/excess liability policies must provide at least the named insured with the same 10-day/30-day notice, unless State law requires otherwise.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when reviewing policy forms/endorsements for required notice language
business_rules.consequences: Meets rule: policy notice language compliant; violated: policy must be endorsed/replaced to meet notice timing
business_rules.exceptions: State law may require different notice; follow State law if more restrictive

source_reference.source_text: Unless required otherwise by State law, each property damage insurance policy must provide that the insurer will notify the named mortgagee in writing at least 10 days before cancellation of the policy by the insurer for nonpayment of the premium or nonrenewal, and at least 30 days before cancellation by the insurer for any other reason. General liability and umbrella or excess liability insurance policies must provide that the insurer will notify at least the named insured in writing at least 10 days before cancellation

examples: A property damage policy provides 10 days’ notice to mortgagee for nonpayment and 30 days for other reasons: compliant.
A liability policy provides 30 days’ notice for any cancellation reason, but no 10-day nonpayment provision: noncompliant; must be corrected.
In a state requiring 45 days’ notice for certain cancellations, the policy must meet the 45-day standard instead of 30.
business_rules.extraction_notes: Explicit notice periods with state-law override.

examples: Property hazard policy with Freddie Mac named as mortgagee/loss payee
Insurance renewal evidence provided prior to policy expiration
Policy change increasing deductible beyond permitted limits

business_rules.rule_name: Initial Insurance Premiums Paid Before Final Loan Delivery
business_rules.description: The Seller/Servicer must ensure the Borrower has paid all initial insurance policy premiums prior to final delivery of the Mortgage to Freddie Mac. The Borrower must pay all renewal/new-policy premiums when due throughout the Mortgage term unless the Servicer collects insurance Reserves under Section 31.2(c). Premium installment/direct bill arrangements are allowed if the Seller/Servicer verifies receipt of payments.
business_rules.conditions: Applies at delivery and throughout servicing for required insurance premiums
business_rules.consequences: Meets rule: delivery/ongoing premium status compliant; violated: must cure (collect/pay) or risk delivery rejection/noncompliance
business_rules.exceptions: Renewal payments may be made from Servicer-collected Reserves per 31.2(c)

source_reference.source_text: The Seller/Servicer must ensure that the Borrower: • Has paid all initial insurance policy premiums prior to final delivery of the Mortgage to Freddie Mac, and • Pays all insurance policy premiums for all renewals or new policies, as applicable, when due throughout the term of the Mortgage, unless the Servicer collects Reserves for insurance in accordance with Section 31.2(c). Premium installment/direct bill arrangements are allowed as long as the Seller/Servicer verifies receipt of payments.

examples: At delivery, the initial property and liability premiums are paid in full and evidenced by receipts: compliant.
Premiums are direct-billed monthly; servicer verifies each installment payment posting: compliant.
Renewal premium is unpaid on due date and no reserve escrow exists: noncompliant; must obtain proof of payment or pay/advance per servicing requirements.
business_rules.extraction_notes: Timing requirements are explicit; limited numeric values but clear triggers (prior to final delivery, when due).

examples: Property hazard policy with Freddie Mac named as mortgagee/loss payee
Insurance renewal evidence provided prior to policy expiration
Policy change increasing deductible beyond permitted limits

business_rules.rule_name: Earthquake Insurance and Retrofit Rules for SEL-475 Above 20%
business_rules.description: Earthquake insurance purchase eligibility and retrofit requirements depend on SEL-475 and whether a building stability concern exists. If SEL-475 ≤ 20% and no building stability concern, earthquake insurance is not required. If a building stability concern exists, the property is ineligible for purchase until seismic retrofit is completed unless otherwise approved by Freddie Mac. If SEL-475 is >20% and ≤40% with no building stability concern, insurance is required (retrofit optional); if retrofit results in SEL-475 ≤20% at completion, insurance will no longer be required. If SEL-475 is >40%, affected building(s) must have a seismic retrofit prior to the Mortgage being submitted to Freddie Mac for consideration.
business_rules.conditions: When determining earthquake insurance/retrofit requirements for a property
business_rules.consequences: If requirements met, mortgage may proceed; if not, mortgage is ineligible for purchase
business_rules.exceptions: Ineligible-until-retrofit may be overridden if otherwise approved by Freddie Mac

source_reference.section_id: Chapter 31 – Insurance Requirements, Required earthquake coverage
source_reference.source_text: Earthquake insurance is required per the table below: SEL-475 Building Stability Concern* No Yes ≤ 20% Insurance not required Ineligible for purchase until seismic retrofit completed unless otherwise approved by Freddie Mac > 20% & ≤ 40% Insurance required, and seismic retrofit optional; if the retrofit results in a SEL-475 ≤ 20% at completion, then insurance will no longer be required Ineligible for purchase until seismic retrofit completed unless otherwise approved by Freddie Mac > 40% The affected building(s) must have a seismic retrofit prior to the Mortgage being submitted to Freddie Mac for consideration

examples: SEL-475 = 18% and no building stability concern: earthquake insurance not required—eligible on this criterion.
SEL-475 = 35% and no stability concern: earthquake insurance required; retrofit optional (if retrofit later reduces SEL-475 to 20%, insurance can cease).
SEL-475 = 25% with building stability concern: mortgage ineligible for purchase until seismic retrofit is completed (unless Freddie approves otherwise).
business_rules.extraction_notes: Table provides explicit thresholds and clear consequence (ineligible for purchase).
business_rules.enforcement_action: Ineligible for purchase until seismic retrofit completed

examples: Property hazard policy with Freddie Mac named as mortgagee/loss payee
Insurance renewal evidence provided prior to policy expiration
Policy change increasing deductible beyond permitted limits

dependencies.rationale: BR_032_007 governs hazard-related eligibility (earthquake insurance/retrofit). BR_018_009 governs counterparty/role-based eligibility via the Exclusionary List. They do not require each other’s data, but both must pass for purchase. Linking them as complementary improves automated ‘eligibility gate’ navigation (e.g., show all failing eligibility reasons).
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If not linked: (1) teams may resolve hazard insurance issues but miss an Exclusionary List hit that still blocks sale; (2) systems may incorrectly mark a loan as ‘cleared for delivery’ after hazard fixes; (3) increased cycle time due to sequential rather than parallel remediation planning.

dependencies.rationale: Zoning/CO compliance and hazard insurance eligibility are separate validations, but they converge on the same operational decision: whether the property/loan is eligible for Freddie Mac purchase. Modeling them as complementary supports comprehensive property eligibility checklists and allows parallel task assignment (legal/zoning vs insurance/hazard).
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If this relationship is not represented: (1) property eligibility may be treated as complete when only one dimension is cleared; (2) remediation plans may be incomplete (e.g., clear zoning but miss retrofit requirement); (3) workflow tools cannot present a consolidated ‘property eligibility’ status.

dependencies.rationale: Property risk and insurance compliance checks are downstream of basic counterparty permissibility. Sanctions is a global prohibition across purchase and servicing-related transactions; it should be evaluated early and gate further underwriting conditions.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If sanctions screening fails, requiring retrofit/insurance changes provides no path to purchase; the file should be terminated or escalated per sanctions policy.

dependencies.rationale: BR_HAZARD_INSURANCE_POLICY_ELIGIBILITY_032_007 requires inputs (SEL-475, building stability concern) that are commonly outputs of seismic risk assessment. Operationally, the compliance workflow should obtain/validate the required SRA documentation before attempting to apply the insurance/retrofit decision logic.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If the SRA is missing/deficient, the lender may be unable to justify why earthquake insurance is or is not required, increasing the risk of purchase ineligibility or post-review findings.

business_rules.rule_name: Earthquake Coverage Must Equal 100% Building IV
business_rules.description: For any property or building where Freddie Mac requires earthquake insurance, the earthquake coverage amount must be at least 100% of the building’s insurable value (IV), plus Business Income/Rental Value coverage per the referenced Guide section.
business_rules.conditions: When Freddie Mac requires earthquake insurance for a property/building
business_rules.consequences: If coverage meets 100% IV minimum, policy satisfies requirement; if below, policy is deficient
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.section_id: Chapter 31 – Insurance Requirements, Required earthquake coverage
source_reference.source_text: Any Property or buildings for which Freddie Mac requires earthquake insurance, the coverage must be at least 100 percent of the IV of the building Plus Business Income/Rental Value coverage per Section 31.6.

examples: Building IV is $6,000,000; earthquake coverage is $6,000,000 and BI/RV endorsement included—compliant.
Building IV is $12,000,000; earthquake coverage is $10,000,000—noncompliant (must be at least $12,000,000).
Earthquake coverage meets 100% IV but BI/RV coverage is omitted—noncompliant per 'Plus Business Income/Rental Value' requirement.
business_rules.extraction_notes: Explicit minimum percentage of IV; BI/RV is an additional required component.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Purchase hold or servicing insurance compliance demand

examples: Property hazard policy with Freddie Mac named as mortgagee/loss payee
Insurance renewal evidence provided prior to policy expiration
Policy change increasing deductible beyond permitted limits
