dependencies.rationale: BR_HAZARD_INSURANCE_POLICY_ELIGIBILITY_032_007 establishes whether Freddie Mac requires earthquake insurance (based on SEL-475 thresholds and building stability concern). Only after that requirement is triggered can the policy coverage-minimum rule (100% of insurable value plus BI/RV coverage) be evaluated.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If eligibility logic is not applied correctly, the workflow may (a) fail to enforce the 100% IV minimum when earthquake insurance is required (purchase ineligibility/QC findings), or (b) impose unnecessary coverage requirements when insurance is not required (borrower cost, closing delays).

business_rules.rule_name: Earthquake Deductible Caps $50k/$100k and 7.5% TIV
business_rules.description: Earthquake deductible maximums apply based on policy limit structure. For Specific Insurance Limits: if expressed as a dollar amount, maximum deductible is $50,000 when IV < $10 million and $100,000 when IV ≥ $10 million. If expressed as a percentage, maximum is 7.5% of the subject property’s TIV with a stated minimum not to exceed $100,000. For Blanket Insurance Limits: if expressed as a dollar amount, maximum is $250,000; if expressed as a percentage, maximum is 7.5% of TIV with a stated minimum not to exceed $250,000. For earthquake business income/rental value coverage, the maximum deductible is 15 days (if in days) or $100,000 (if in dollars).
business_rules.conditions: When evaluating earthquake and BI/RV deductibles on hazard insurance policies
business_rules.consequences: If deductibles within caps, policy is compliant; if above, policy must be amended/replaced
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.source_text: When expressed as a dollar amount: IV of the Property Maximum deductible < $10 million $50,000 ≥ $10 million $100,000 • When expressed as a percentage, 7.5 percent of the subject property’s TIV, with a stated minimum not to exceed $100,000 For Blanket Insurance Limits: • When expressed as a dollar amount, $250,000 • When expressed as a percentage, 7.5 percent of the subject property’s TIV, with a stated minimum not to exceed $250,000 The maximum deductible for earthquake business income/rental value coverage is as follows: • When expressed as number of days, 15 days • When expressed as a dollar amount, $100,000

examples: Specific limits, IV $9.5M, deductible $50,000—compliant; deductible $75,000—noncompliant.
Specific limits, deductible 7.5% of TIV with minimum $125,000—noncompliant (minimum must not exceed $100,000).
Blanket limits with BI/RV deductible of 20 days—noncompliant (max 15 days).
business_rules.extraction_notes: Multiple explicit caps by structure; highly testable thresholds.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Purchase hold or servicing insurance compliance demand

examples: Property hazard policy with Freddie Mac named as mortgagee/loss payee
Insurance renewal evidence provided prior to policy expiration
Policy change increasing deductible beyond permitted limits

dependencies.rationale: BR_032_009 governs whether a presented earthquake policy meets deductible limits. That evaluation is only actionable when BR_032_007 indicates earthquake coverage is required for purchase eligibility (or when the lender chooses to include it in response to BR_032_007 triggers).
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_032_007 is not applied first, deductible compliance may be checked unnecessarily or missed entirely, causing either avoidable policy rework or purchase ineligibility due to noncompliant deductibles.

dependencies.rationale: Coverage adequacy and deductible permissibility are separate but jointly necessary conditions of an acceptable earthquake policy. In an automated compliance checklist, both checks should be executed as a combined policy-compliance set.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_032_008 is satisfied but BR_032_009 fails (or vice versa), the earthquake insurance requirement is not met, leading to underwriting/closing conditions and potential purchase ineligibility.

business_rules.rule_name: Wind and Hail Deductible Max $50k or $100k
business_rules.description: For Wind/Hail coverage under Specific Insurance Limits, the maximum deductible is capped based on Insurable Value (IV): if IV of the Property is < $10 million, the maximum deductible is $50,000; if IV is ≥ $10 million, the maximum deductible is $100,000.
business_rules.conditions: When Wind/Hail deductible is a dollar amount under Specific Limits
business_rules.consequences: Meets cap: acceptable; exceeds cap: policy noncompliant and must be corrected
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.source_text: b. Wind/ Hail deductibles ( 12/16/25) For Specific Insurance Limit s: • When expressed as a dollar amount: IV of the Property Maximum deductible < $10 million $50,000 ≥ $10 million $100,000 • When expressed as a percentage, five percent of the subject property’s TIV with a stated minimum not to exceed $100,000

examples: Property IV $9,500,000 and Wind/Hail deductible $50,000: compliant (≤ $50,000).
Property IV $10,000,000 and deductible $100,000: compliant (≤ $100,000).
Property IV $12,000,000 and deductible $150,000: noncompliant; revise policy terms to ≤ $100,000.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Purchase hold / delivery deficiency until corrected

examples: Property hazard policy with Freddie Mac named as mortgagee/loss payee
Insurance renewal evidence provided prior to policy expiration
Policy change increasing deductible beyond permitted limits

business_rules.rule_name: Wind and Hail Deductible Cap 5% TIV Max $100k
business_rules.description: For Wind/Hail coverage under Specific Insurance Limits where the deductible is expressed as a percentage, the deductible must be five percent (5%) of the subject property's Total Insurable Value (TIV), with a stated minimum not to exceed $100,000.
business_rules.conditions: When Wind/Hail deductible is expressed as a percentage under Specific Limits
business_rules.consequences: Meets cap: acceptable; stated minimum > $100,000: policy noncompliant
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.source_text: IV of the Property Maximum deductible < $10 million $50,000 ≥ $10 million $100,000 • When expressed as a percentage, five percent of the subject property’s TIV with a stated minimum not to exceed $100,000

examples: TIV $3,000,000; 5% deductible = $150,000 but stated minimum must not exceed $100,000: noncompliant if the policy uses a $150,000 minimum.
TIV $8,000,000; deductible 5% with stated minimum $75,000: compliant (minimum ≤ $100,000).
TIV $20,000,000; deductible 5% with stated minimum $125,000: noncompliant; reduce minimum to ≤ $100,000.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Purchase hold / delivery deficiency until corrected

examples: Property hazard policy with Freddie Mac named as mortgagee/loss payee
Insurance renewal evidence provided prior to policy expiration
Policy change increasing deductible beyond permitted limits

business_rules.rule_name: Wind and Hail BI Deductible Max 15 Days or $100k
business_rules.description: For Wind/Hail business income/rental value coverage, the maximum deductible is capped at 15 days when expressed as days, or $100,000 when expressed as a dollar amount.
business_rules.conditions: When Wind/Hail business income/rental value coverage is included
business_rules.consequences: Meets cap: acceptable; exceeds cap: policy noncompliant and must be revised
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.source_text: five percent of the subject property’s TIV, with a stated minimum not to exceed $250,000 The maximum deductible for Wind/Hail business income/rental value coverage is: • When expressed as number of days, 15 days • When expressed as a dollar amount, $100,000

examples: Policy lists BI waiting period deductible of 10 days: compliant (≤ 15 days).
Policy lists BI deductible of 20 days: noncompliant; must be reduced to 15 days or less.
Policy lists BI deductible $150,000: noncompliant; must be reduced to ≤ $100,000.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Purchase hold / delivery deficiency until corrected

examples: Property hazard policy with Freddie Mac named as mortgagee/loss payee
Insurance renewal evidence provided prior to policy expiration
Policy change increasing deductible beyond permitted limits

business_rules.rule_name: Named Storm Blanket Limit Minimum 90% of Largest TIV
business_rules.description: If Named Storm coverage is provided as part of a Blanket Insurance Limit, the blanket limit may not be less than 90% of the largest individual TIV covered by the policy. The blanket limit must be per-occurrence and must reinstate to the required limit after any losses.
business_rules.conditions: When Named Storm coverage is provided as a Blanket Insurance Limit
business_rules.consequences: Meets limit/reinstatement: acceptable; otherwise: inadequate Named Storm limits
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.section_id: Chapter 31, Named Storm blanket limit (Page 14, 12/16/25 update)
source_reference.source_text: The Blanket Insurance Limit for Named Storm may not be less than 90 percent of the largest individual TIV covered by the policy. The blanket limit must be per-occurrence and reinstate to the required limit after any losses.

examples: Largest TIV among covered properties is $20MM; blanket Named Storm limit must be ≥ $18MM (90%): $18MM is compliant.
Largest TIV is $20MM; blanket limit $15MM: noncompliant (75% < 90%).
Blanket is ≥90% but is aggregate (not per-occurrence): noncompliant; must be per-occurrence.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Purchase hold / delivery deficiency until corrected

examples: Property hazard policy with Freddie Mac named as mortgagee/loss payee
Insurance renewal evidence provided prior to policy expiration
Policy change increasing deductible beyond permitted limits

business_rules.rule_name: Named Storm Deductible 7.5% TIV with $50k $100k Caps
business_rules.description: Named Storm deductible limits apply. For Specific Insurance Limits: dollar deductibles capped at $50,000 when IV < $10 million and $100,000 when IV ≥ $10 million; percent deductibles set at 7.5% of TIV with stated minimum not to exceed $100,000. For Blanket Insurance Limits: dollar deductible $250,000; percent deductible 7.5% of TIV with stated minimum not to exceed $250,000. Named Storm business income/rental value deductible is capped at 15 days or $100,000.
business_rules.conditions: When Named Storm coverage is required and deductibles apply
business_rules.consequences: Meets caps: acceptable; exceeds caps: noncompliant deductible terms
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.source_text: d. Named Storm deductibles ( 12/16/25) For Specific Insurance Limit s: • When expressed as a dollar amount: IV of the Property Maximum deductible < $10 million $50,000 ≥ $10 million $100,000 • When expressed as a percentage, 7.5 percent of the subject property’s TIV, with a stated minimum not to exceed $100,000 For Blanket Insurance Limit s: • When expressed as a dollar amount, $250,000 • When expressed as a percentage, 7.5 percent of the subject property’s TIV, with a stated minimum not to exceed $250,000 The maximum deductible for Named Storm business income/rental value coverage is: • When expressed as number of days, 15 days • When expressed as a dollar amount, $100,000

examples: IV $9MM with Named Storm deductible $60,000: noncompliant (must be ≤ $50,000 for IV < $10MM).
Blanket Named Storm deductible $250,000: compliant (dollar deductible equals the allowed max).
Named Storm BI deductible 30 days: noncompliant (must be ≤ 15 days).
business_rules.enforcement_action: Purchase hold / delivery deficiency until corrected

examples: Property hazard policy with Freddie Mac named as mortgagee/loss payee
Insurance renewal evidence provided prior to policy expiration
Policy change increasing deductible beyond permitted limits

dependencies.rationale: These rules jointly define Named Storm acceptability: BR_037_004 ensures the blanket limit is not under-sized and reinstates after losses, while BR_037_005 ensures deductibles are within Freddie Mac caps. Together they form a cohesive Named Storm compliance set.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If either rule fails, Named Storm insurance terms are noncompliant, risking insurance conditions, delayed delivery/purchase, or forced mid-process policy replacement.

business_rules.rule_name: State Windpool Requires 100% TIV and ≤80% Coinsurance
business_rules.description: If windstorm coverage is only available from a state-sponsored insurance program or State Windpool, the policy must be written in an amount no less than 100% of the TIV and meet deductible requirements. If the policy contains a coinsurance clause, it must either include an agreed amount provision equal to no less than 100% of the IV of the improvements, or contain a coinsurance clause less than or equal to 80%.
business_rules.conditions: When windstorm coverage is only available via State Windpool program
business_rules.consequences: Meets limits/coinsurance rules: acceptable; otherwise: policy must be corrected
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.section_id: Chapter 31, Section 31.7(e) Windstorm insurance through a State Windpool (12/16/25)
source_reference.source_text: If windstorm coverage is only available from a state -sponsored insurance program or State Windpool, the policy must: • Be written in an amount no less than 100 percent of the TIV and meet the deductible requirements in this Section 31. 7. • If the policy contains a Coinsurance Clause, it must: o Include an Agreed Amount Provision equal to no less than 100 percent of the IV of the improvements, or o Contain a Coinsurance Clause less than or equal to 80 percent

examples: State Windpool policy limit equals 100% TIV and coinsurance 80%: compliant.
State Windpool policy limit equals 90% TIV: noncompliant; increase limit to ≥100% TIV.
Coinsurance is 90% and no agreed amount provision: noncompliant; add agreed amount ≥100% IV improvements or reduce coinsurance to ≤80%.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Purchase hold / delivery deficiency until corrected

examples: Property hazard policy with Freddie Mac named as mortgagee/loss payee
Insurance renewal evidence provided prior to policy expiration
Policy change increasing deductible beyond permitted limits

dependencies.rationale: When coverage is only available through a State Windpool, BR_037_006 imposes minimum limit and deductible compliance. BR_037_001 is one of the deductible cap rules needed to determine if the submitted State Windpool policy is acceptable (for dollar-amount deductibles under Specific Limits).
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_037_001 is not applied, a State Windpool policy could be accepted with an excessive deductible, creating a noncompliant insurance file and potential Freddie Mac remedies/conditions.

dependencies.rationale: To confirm a State Windpool policy meets Freddie Mac deductible requirements, the system must evaluate percentage-based deductibles using BR_037_002 when that deductible structure is used.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_037_002 is skipped, a policy could be accepted with a noncompliant stated minimum (e.g., minimum > $100,000), triggering post-review exceptions and policy rework.

dependencies.rationale: BR_037_006 broadly requires meeting deductible requirements. If the State Windpool policy includes business income/rental value coverage, BR_037_003 supplies the deductible cap for that component and should be evaluated to complete compliance.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If not evaluated, the file may pass initial review but fail later audit/QC if BI/RV deductible exceeds 15 days or $100,000, requiring endorsement changes.

business_rules.rule_name: SFHA Flood Coverage Includes 12 Months Business Income
business_rules.description: For each income-generating building fully or partially located in a SFHA, required flood insurance must be at least: (i) IV of first two floors above grade, plus (ii) IV of floors below grade, plus (iii) 12 months of business income/rental value for the building, and (iv) IV of Borrower-owned contents/business personal property within the building.
business_rules.conditions: When an income-generating building is fully/partially in a SFHA (Zone A or V)
business_rules.consequences: Meets minimum formula: acceptable; below minimum: insufficient flood coverage
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.source_text: For each income -generating building that is fully or partially located in a SFHA, Freddie Mac requires flood insurance equal to at least the following: • The IV of the first two floors of the building above grade, plus • The IV of any floors below grade, plus • 12 months of business income/rental value associated with the building, and • The IV of Borrower -owned contents or business personal property within the building.

examples: Building has IV first two floors $6MM, below-grade IV $1MM, BI 12 months $0.8MM, contents $0.2MM: minimum required = $8.0MM total.
Policy covers $6.5MM total for same building: noncompliant (short of $8.0MM minimum).
Building has no below-grade floors (IV below grade $0): minimum excludes that component but still must include 12 months BI and contents.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Purchase hold / delivery deficiency until corrected

examples: Property hazard policy with Freddie Mac named as mortgagee/loss payee
Insurance renewal evidence provided prior to policy expiration
Policy change increasing deductible beyond permitted limits

business_rules.rule_name: Non-consolidation opinion required at $40 million UPB
business_rules.description: For non-SBL Mortgages, the Final Delivery Package must include a Non-Consolidation Opinion for any Mortgage (except a TEL Mortgage, excluding split supplemental TEL Mortgages) with an original principal balance ≥ $40,000,000, or that is part of a cross-collateralized/cross-defaulted pool aggregated to ≥ $40,000,000, or if otherwise required by the Letter of Commitment or early rate lock application.
business_rules.conditions: Non-SBL Mortgage final delivery; original/prior pool balance triggers ≥$40M
business_rules.consequences: If required opinion is missing, delivery package is incomplete and may be conditioned/rejected
business_rules.exceptions: TEL Mortgage excluded (except split supplemental TEL Mortgages)

source_reference.source_text:
• A non -consolidation legal opinion (the “Non -Consolidation Opinion” ) for any Mortgage , except for a TEL Mortgage (this exclusion does not include split supplemental  TEL  Mortgages ):

o With an original principal balance equal to or greater than $40,000,000;

o That is a part of a cross -collateralized and cross -defaulted pool of Mortgages that are, when aggregated, $40,000,000 or greater; or

o If otherwise required by the Letter of Commitment or early rate lock application

examples: A $55,000,000 non-SBL Mortgage (not TEL) must include a Non-Consolidation Opinion in the Final Delivery Package.
Two cross-collateralized loans of $22,000,000 and $21,000,000 (aggregate $43,000,000) require a Non-Consolidation Opinion for each Mortgage in the pool.
A $45,000,000 TEL Mortgage does not require a Non-Consolidation Opinion unless it is a split supplemental TEL Mortgage.
Complete closing package including recorded security instrument and final title policy
Closing package pending receipt of updated insurance certificate
Digitally stored closing package in an approved eVault

dependencies.rationale: A Non-Consolidation Opinion is a final delivery requirement for certain non-SBL transactions. However, if the deal is prohibited under sanctions screening, the delivery process should stop before document assembly/review to avoid operational waste and reduce legal risk.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If sanctions screening fails after preparing the opinion, the lender incurs unnecessary legal cost and time; automated workflow should gate document requests on a cleared sanctions status.

dependencies.rationale: When an opinion is required for delivery, it often must be drafted and (for certain high-balance/special opinions) submitted for Freddie review prior to origination. This creates an operational sequence: submit opinion(s) for review early → later include final executed opinion in delivery package. Automations can prompt early submission based on the same trigger set (loan size/type/opinion type) to prevent downstream delivery defects.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_012_005 is missed when applicable, origination/delivery may be delayed or conditioned, increasing the risk that the Final Delivery Package will be incomplete or not accepted on the expected timeline under BR_012_004.

dependencies.rationale: While a lender could technically assemble a Final Delivery Package even if an SCP match exists, in an automated workflow the SCP screen should occur before (or at least concurrently with) costly legal deliverables like Non-Consolidation Opinions. This enables efficient rule navigation: if BR_035_002 fails, BR_012_004 becomes non-actionable for a purchase path.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_035_002 fails late (after delivery package prepared), significant legal cost and cycle-time are wasted (e.g., obtaining Non-Consolidation Opinions), and delivery will still be rejected/denied.

business_rules.rule_name: Annual Financial Rent Roll Age Maximum 6 Months
business_rules.description: For Annual Financial submissions, the rent roll must be current: dated no more than six months prior to the Annual Financial submission date and, for purposes of Annual Financial completeness, dated no more than six months prior to the original due date (e.g., due June 1 requires rent roll dated on/after Dec 1 of the prior year).
business_rules.conditions: Applies when Servicer submits Annual Financial for a Freddie Mac-held Mortgage
business_rules.consequences: If met, rent roll satisfies currency; if violated, Annual Financial may be deemed incomplete
business_rules.exceptions: None

source_reference.source_text: The rent roll must be no older than six months prior to the Annual Financial submission date. ... The Servicer must submit all of the following components of the Annual Financial via PRS in order for Freddie Mac to deem the submission of the Annual Financial to be complete: ... 3. Current rent roll, identifying commercial units where applicable, certified by the Borrower ... For the purposes of the Annual Financial, a rent roll is considered to be current if it is dated no more than six months prior to the original due date. For example, if the Annual Financial due date is June 1, the rent roll is considered current if dated on or after December 1 of the prior year.

examples: Meets rule: Annual Financial due June 1; rent roll dated Dec 15 prior year is acceptable (within 6 months).
Fails rule: Annual Financial due June 1; rent roll dated Nov 30 prior year is stale (older than 6 months).
Meets rule: Submission date Aug 15; rent roll dated Mar 1 is within 6 months of submission.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Freddie Mac deems Annual Financial submission incomplete

examples: Monthly servicing report indicating 30 days delinquent status
Servicing report reflecting partial payment and suspense handling
Corrected servicing report resubmitted after reconciliation

business_rules.rule_name: Annual Financial Assessment Waiver Under 6 or 3 Months Data
business_rules.description: If the Borrower cannot provide an Income and Expense Statement with 12 months of data for the immediate preceding fiscal year, the Servicer may accept a partial-year statement. For non-SBL Mortgages, the assessment may be waived if less than six months of data is available; for SBL Mortgages, the assessment may be waived if less than three months of data is available.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when Annual Financial source financials lack 12 months of prior fiscal data
business_rules.consequences: If thresholds met, Servicer may waive assessment; otherwise assessment required
business_rules.exceptions: None beyond stated non-SBL (<6 months) and SBL (<3 months) thresholds

source_reference.source_text: The Property’s Income and Expense Statement must be for the immediate preceding fiscal year. If the Borrower cannot provide an Income and Expense Statement with 12 months of data, the Servicer may accept a partial-year statement from the Borrower. For non-SBL Mortgages, the assessment may be waived if less than six months of data is available from the Borrower. For SBL Mortgages, the assessment may be waived if less than three months of data is available from the Borrower.

examples: Non-SBL: Borrower provides only 5 months of operations; Servicer may waive the assessment (<6 months).
SBL: Borrower provides 2 months of operations post-acquisition; Servicer may waive the assessment (<3 months).
Non-SBL: Borrower provides 8 months of data; assessment may not be waived under this rule.
Monthly servicing report indicating 30 days delinquent status
Servicing report reflecting partial payment and suspense handling
Corrected servicing report resubmitted after reconciliation

business_rules.rule_name: Zoning Opinion Required When Loan 20MM or 25MM and No Zoning Report
business_rules.description: When a third-party zoning consulting report is not submitted because the Mortgage is (i) a non-SBL Mortgage with an initial principal balance of $20 million or less, or (ii) a Supplemental Mortgage where the combined initial principal balance of the Supplemental Mortgage plus unpaid principal balances of senior Mortgages is $25 million or less in the aggregate, and the Mortgage is not a Moderate Rehabilitation, Lease-up, Value-add, or Forward Commitment Mortgage, the appraiser must include an opinion as to the legality of the Property’s zoning (Legal, Legally non-conforming, Illegal, or Other with explanation). The preferred method is quoting or referencing an appropriate local zoning office official/employee; without such quote/reference, the appraiser must provide a professional opinion and cannot merely state they are not experts or that the client should consult a legal expert.
business_rules.conditions: When no zoning report is submitted and Mortgage fits $20MM/$25MM criteria
business_rules.consequences: If met: underwriting has zoning legality; if violated: appraisal deficiency/revision
business_rules.exceptions: Does not apply if Mortgage is Moderate Rehab/Lease-up/Value-add/Forward Commit

source_reference.section_id: Chapter 60 f. Zoning and other legal issues (06/25/20)
source_reference.source_text: If a Mortgage meets either of the following conditions, a third -party zoning consulting report might not be part of the underwriting package... o Is a non -SBL Mortgage with an initial principal balance of $20 million or less, or is a Supplemental Mortgage and the combined initial principal balance of the Supplemental Mortgage and the unpaid principal balances of any senior Mortgages encumbering the Property are $25 million or less in the aggregate, and o Is not a Moderate Rehabilitation Mortgage, a Lease -up Mortgage, a Value -add Mortgage or a Forward Commitment Mortgage... the appraiser must include an opinion as to the legality of the zoning of the Property... The appraiser cannot merely state that they are not experts in this field or that the client should consult a legal expert.

examples: Non-SBL $18,000,000 refinance with no zoning report: appraisal must state zoning is legal/legally non-conforming/illegal and support it.
Supplemental Mortgage $5,000,000 with $19,000,000 senior UPB (combined $24,000,000): zoning opinion is required if no zoning report is submitted.
Lease-up Mortgage $15,000,000 with no zoning report: this exception applies; the special $20MM condition does not trigger the same instruction.
MAI appraisal supporting a $35M value conclusion
Update appraisal required due to aging report date at closing
Appraisal with extraordinary assumptions disclosed

business_rules.rule_name: Sales comparison approach requires at least 4 comparable sales plus 4 if subject used
business_rules.description: When supporting value using the sales comparison approach, the appraiser must analyze sales of at least four comparable properties. The appraiser may use the subject property as a comparable sale only if the appraiser provides four additional comparables to the property.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when Appraisal includes a sales comparison approach value indication
business_rules.consequences: If four+ comparable sales analyzed, approach meets minimum Guide requirement; if fewer, Appraisal is deficient and may be rejected/conditioned
business_rules.exceptions: Subject property may be used only with four additional comparables

source_reference.source_text: The appraiser must support the value indicated by the sales comparison approach by analyzing the sales of at least four comparable properties. The appraiser may use the Property as a comparable sale as long as the appraiser provides four additional comparables to the Property.

examples: Appraiser includes only three comparable sales: Appraisal does not meet the minimum and must be supplemented with at least one more comparable sale.
Appraiser analyzes four comparable sales and does not use the subject as a comparable: requirement is met.
Appraiser uses the subject as a comparable sale: Appraisal must include four additional comparable sales (total at least five sales analyzed with subject included).
business_rules.extraction_notes: Explicit minimum count (four) and conditional rule when subject is used.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Purchase eligibility condition / appraisal revision required

examples: MAI appraisal supporting a $35M value conclusion
Update appraisal required due to aging report date at closing
Appraisal with extraordinary assumptions disclosed

dependencies.rationale: Underwriting typically treats appraisal completeness as a bundle of required components. BR_035_005 ensures zoning compliance support is present when a zoning report is absent; BR_038_006 ensures sales comparison methodology meets minimum comparable count. Together they form a practical appraisal QC checklist for acceptance/conditioning.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If either requirement fails, the appraisal may be conditioned/rejected, delaying underwriting approval and potentially closing timelines.

business_rules.rule_name: Conversion Restriction Term Must Be No Longer Than 10 Years
business_rules.description: If there is a prohibition against converting the Property to a condominium or cooperative structure (or an indemnification relating to such conversion), the agreement/restriction must meet specified conditions including that the term of the agreement/restriction and any obligations contained in it must be no longer than 10 years.
business_rules.conditions: Recorded/unrecorded agreement restricts condo/co-op conversion or includes conversion indemnity
business_rules.consequences: If term ≤10 years, restriction term condition satisfied; if >10 years, Seller/Servicer must cure/modify or seek Freddie acceptance
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.source_text: If there is a prohibition against the conversion of the Property to a condominium or cooperative structure or any indemnification by an owner of the Property relating to the conversion of the Property to a condominium or cooperative structure, then the agreement/restriction must meet the following conditions:  a. Other than the restriction prohibiting the conversion of the Property into a condominium or cooperative development, there may be no other restrictions on the use or development of the Property in the agreement/restriction.  b. The term of the agreement/restriction and any obligations contained in it must be no longer than 10 years.

examples: A recorded conversion restriction runs for 8 years and contains no other use restrictions: satisfies the 10-year term condition.
A restriction prohibits conversion for 15 years: noncompliant; must be amended to ≤10 years or otherwise addressed for Freddie acceptance.
A restriction is 5 years but also restricts redevelopment: fails condition (a) even though term is ≤10 years.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Purchase eligibility condition / legal documentation cure required

examples: Deed of trust recorded in county land records
Mortgage security instrument with first-lien position
Security instrument amended via recorded modification

business_rules.rule_name: Accounting cutoff date is close of business on the 15th
business_rules.description: For accounting and reporting purposes, the accounting cutoff date is defined as the close of business on the 15th of a month. If the 15th is not a Business Day, the cutoff date is the close of business on the Business Day preceding the 15th.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when determining cutoff date for accounting/reporting under Chapter 50
business_rules.consequences: Correct cutoff date aligns reporting period; incorrect cutoff may misstate transmissions
business_rules.exceptions: If 15th not Business Day, use preceding Business Day close of business

source_reference.source_text: 50.8 Definitions for accounting and reporting ( 02/14/22) As used in this chapter, these terms are defined as follows: 1. Accounting cutoff date . The close of business on the 15th of a month . (If the 15th is not a Business Day, the accounting cutoff date is the close of business on the Business Day preceding the 15th of the month.)

examples: If the 15th falls on a Wednesday Business Day: cutoff is COB on the 15th.
If the 15th falls on a Saturday: cutoff is COB on the preceding Friday Business Day.
A report uses the 16th as cutoff: violates the defined accounting cutoff date.
Monthly accounting report where accounting net yield differs due to purchase premium
Reconciliation report identifying remittance variance
Exception report for incorrect net yield setup

business_rules.rule_name: Accounting cycle runs from the 16th through the 15th
business_rules.description: For accounting and reporting purposes, the accounting cycle is defined as the 16th of one month through the 15th of the following month.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to defining monthly accounting reporting coverage under Chapter 50
business_rules.consequences: Correct cycle ensures complete loan-level reporting; misaligned cycles create omissions
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.source_text: 2. Accounting cycle . The 16th of one month through the 15th of the following month .

examples: Cycle for May/June: May 16 through June 15.
Loan activity on June 14 belongs to the cycle ending June 15.
Reporting June 1–June 30 as the accounting cycle would not match the defined cycle.
Monthly accounting report where accounting net yield differs due to purchase premium
Reconciliation report identifying remittance variance
Exception report for incorrect net yield setup

dependencies.rationale: BR_039_005 defines the operational cutoff date used for accounting/reporting. BR_039_006 defines the cycle as 16th through 15th. Systems typically must implement the cutoff date logic (including business-day adjustment) to correctly operationalize the cycle close.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If cutoff-date logic is not applied, cycle boundaries can be misapplied (especially when the 15th is not a business day), causing misstatement of monthly accounting transmissions.

business_rules.rule_name: Exception activity means UPB reduced to zero by defined events
business_rules.description: For accounting and reporting purposes, exception activity is defined as activity resulting in the Mortgage unpaid principal balance (UPB) being reduced to zero. Transactions that are exception activities include Mortgages paid in full or repurchased, liquidated by third-party foreclosure sale, transferred to REO, and sold into a Securitization.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when classifying and reporting zero-UPB events in accounting transmissions
business_rules.consequences: Correct classification triggers required exception reporting; misclassification may omit reporting
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.source_text: 3. Exception activity . Activity resulting in the unpaid principal balance (UPB) of a Mortgage being reduced to zero . The following transactions are exception activities: • Mortgages paid in full or repurchased (Sections 54.3 and 54.4) • Mortgages liquidated by third -party foreclosure sale (Section 54.5) • Mortgages transferred to REO (Section 54.6) • Mortgages sold into a Securitization

examples: Mortgage paid in full: UPB becomes $0 and must be treated as exception activity.
Third-party foreclosure sale liquidates the Mortgage: UPB becomes $0 and is exception activity.
Regular scheduled amortization reduces UPB but not to $0: not exception activity under this definition.
Monthly accounting report where accounting net yield differs due to purchase premium
Reconciliation report identifying remittance variance
Exception report for incorrect net yield setup

dependencies.rationale: BR_039_007 defines what qualifies as exception activity (UPB reduced to zero events). BR_039_006 defines the monthly cycle window used for reporting. To report exception activities accurately, the system must place the event into the correct cycle period.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If cycle alignment is missing, exception activity could be reported in the wrong month or omitted from required cycle transmissions.

business_rules.rule_name: Adverse Action Notice Required Within 30 Calendar Days After Decline
business_rules.description: The Seller/Servicer must issue the Borrower an Adverse Action Notice within 30 calendar days when (i) Freddie Mac declines to purchase a new Mortgage loan or a post-closing extension of credit, and (ii) for loan originations, the Borrower does not accept the Seller’s quote from another institution offering similar credit terms. This is a timing requirement tied to ECOA adverse action notifications.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when Freddie Mac declines purchase and borrower does not accept alternate quote
business_rules.consequences: If met, notice timing is compliant; if violated, ECOA/Guide noncompliance risk escalates
business_rules.exceptions: Non-issuance allowed only if documented as ECOA-compliant (separate requirement)

source_reference.section_id: Guide Chapter 10 (02/24/26 update), Adverse Action Notice timing
source_reference.source_text: The Seller /Servicer must issue t he Borrower an Adverse Action Notice within 30 calendar days if : • Freddie Mac declines to purchase a new Mortgage loan or a post -closing extension of credit (e.g., a Transfer of Ownership/Assumption ; refer to the FAQs on Adverse Action Notices for the entire list ), and • For loan originations, t he Borrower does not accept the Seller’s quote from another institution offering similar credit terms

examples: Freddie Mac declines purchase on June 1; Seller/Servicer must issue the adverse action notice no later than July 1 (30 calendar days).
Post-closing assumption request is declined by Freddie Mac; Seller/Servicer must send adverse action notice within 30 calendar days of the decline.
Borrower accepts a comparable quote from another institution immediately; this specific trigger condition is not satisfied, so the 30-day issuance requirement may not apply under this clause.
Seller/Servicer sends written adverse action notice after denial
Seller/Servicer provides oral notice followed by required written disclosures
Adverse action notice delivered with stated reasons consistent with underwriting file

business_rules.rule_name: Upload Adverse Action Evidence to DMS Within 5 Business Days Effective 4/1/2025
business_rules.description: Effective April 1, 2025, the Seller/Servicer must upload into DMS (Adverse Action Notices folder) within five Business Days following issuance to the Borrower either: (i) a copy of the written Adverse Action Notice and evidence of issuance (e.g., email, certified mail receipt, courier receipt), or (ii) a written statement documenting the date an oral Adverse Action Notice was issued and confirming ECOA-required information was conveyed. For Mortgage loan originations, if no notice was issued, a written statement explaining why non-issuance complies with ECOA is required.
business_rules.conditions: Applies on/after 2025-04-01, within 5 Business Days after issuing oral/written notice
business_rules.consequences: If not uploaded timely/complete, DMS file is noncompliant and may trigger QC findings
business_rules.exceptions: Non-issuance permitted only with ECOA-compliance statement in file

source_reference.section_id: Guide Chapter 10 (02/24/26 update), DMS upload requirement
source_reference.source_text: Additionally, effective April 1, 2025, the Seller /Servicer must upload into DMS in the Adverse Action Notices folder (in accordance with the DMS Job Aid for Uploading Adverse Action Notices) within five Business Days following issuance of the Adverse Action Notice to the Borrower : • A copy of a written Adverse Action Notice and evidence that the Seller /Servicer issued it to the Borrower (e.g., email, certified mail receipt, courier receipt, etc.); or • A written statement detailing the date that the Seller /Servicer issued an oral Adverse Action Notice

examples: Written notice emailed on May 6, 2025: Seller/Servicer must upload the notice and email evidence to DMS by the 5th Business Day after May 6.
Oral notice given by phone on June 10, 2025: Seller/Servicer uploads a written statement with the issuance date and ECOA confirmation within 5 Business Days.
Seller/Servicer determines no adverse action notice is required for an origination: must upload a written ECOA-compliance explanation statement into DMS.
Seller/Servicer sends written adverse action notice after denial
Seller/Servicer provides oral notice followed by required written disclosures
Adverse action notice delivered with stated reasons consistent with underwriting file

dependencies.rationale: BR_001_001 governs the requirement to issue an Adverse Action Notice within 30 days. BR_001_002 requires the Seller/Servicer to upload evidence/copies to DMS within five Business Days following that issuance. The documentation clock and artifact depend on the issuance event.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_001_001 is missed or date is wrong, BR_001_002 cannot be timed correctly; this creates ECOA timing violations and missing DMS evidence, increasing regulatory exposure and QC findings.

business_rules.rule_name: SBL default date uses failure date 46SBL.6(d)(2)
business_rules.description: For SBL Loss calculation purposes, if the Loan Documents provide a grace or cure period for a Borrower failure, the Mortgage Default is deemed to have occurred on the date of the failure (not the end of the grace or cure period). This deemed default date governs SBL Loss timing components (e.g., interest accrual start).
business_rules.conditions: Applies when Loan Documents include grace/cure and SBL Loss is calculated
business_rules.consequences: If grace/cure exists, use failure date for default date; using cure-end date is noncompliant
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.source_text: For purposes of calculation of the amount of the loss for an SBL Mortgage (“SBL Loss”), if the Loan Documents provide a grace or cure period with respect to a failure by the Borrower to comply with one or more of its obligations, the Mortgage Default will be deemed to have occurred on the date of the failure, not the end of the grace or cure period.

examples: Borrower misses a payment due 05/01; note allows 10-day cure → Mortgage Default deemed 05/01 for SBL Loss timing.
Borrower fails to maintain insurance on 03/10; cure period ends 03/25 → default deemed 03/10 for SBL Loss.
Servicer starts default-interest calculation from cure-end date → incorrect; must start from failure date.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loss obligation recalculation and corrective action

examples: Servicer collects monthly payments and remits interest and principal per remittance rules
Servicer monitors insurance renewals and enforces coverage requirements
Servicer initiates default management after sustained delinquency

business_rules.rule_name: SBL loss equals UPB plus costs plus interest minus recoveries
business_rules.description: SBL Loss equals (i) unpaid principal balance of the Defaulted Mortgage as of the Mortgage Default, plus (ii) Default or Modification Resolution Costs as of the SBL Loss Obligation Calculation Date, plus (iii) interest due from Borrower under the Loan Documents from the Mortgage Default date until the SBL Loss Obligation Calculation Date not otherwise included in costs, minus any applicable Default Recoveries or Modification Recoveries.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when Freddie Mac calculates SBL Loss on/after a Mortgage Default
business_rules.consequences: If calculated, forms basis of SBL Loss Obligation (subject to Loss Maximum); errors require correction
business_rules.exceptions: Subtract applicable Default Recoveries or Modification Recoveries

source_reference.source_text: The terms “SBL Loss” or “SBL Losses” mean the sum of each of the following, minus any applicable Default Recoveries or Modification Recoveries: • The unpaid principal balance of the Defaulted Mortgage as of the Mortgage Default • Default or Modification Resolution Costs as of the SBL Loss Obligation Calculation Date • Interest due from Borrower under the Loan Documents from the date of the Mortgage Default until the SBL Loss Obligation Calculation Date not otherwise included in the calculation of Default or Modification Resolution Costs

examples: UPB at default $1,200,000 + costs $80,000 + interest $20,000 − recoveries $150,000 = SBL Loss $1,150,000.
No recoveries yet: UPB $900,000 + costs $25,000 + interest $8,000 = SBL Loss $933,000.
If some interest is included in resolution costs, exclude that portion from the separate interest add-on.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loss obligation payment demand and reconciliation

examples: Servicer collects monthly payments and remits interest and principal per remittance rules
Servicer monitors insurance renewals and enforces coverage requirements
Servicer initiates default management after sustained delinquency

dependencies.rationale: BR_002_003 establishes the deemed default date (date of failure, not cure-end) for SBL loss timing components. BR_002_004 explicitly calculates interest “from the Mortgage Default date until the… Calculation Date,” making BR_002_003 a necessary upstream determiner of inputs to BR_002_004.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_002_003 is not applied, interest accrual timing in BR_002_004 can be misstated, causing incorrect SBL Loss amounts, billing/settlement disputes, and required recalculations.

business_rules.rule_name: Late charge waiver barred for ARM or SBL Hybrid ARM 43.7
business_rules.description: A Servicer may not waive collection of any late charge payable under the Note for an ARM or an SBL Hybrid ARM.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when Borrower requests late charge waiver on ARM/SBL Hybrid ARM
business_rules.consequences: If ARM/SBL Hybrid ARM, waiver is not permitted; late charge must be collected
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.source_text: The Servicer must document the waiver and retain evidence of the waiver in the Mortgage File. The Servicer may not waive collection of any late charge payable under the Note for an ARM, or an SBL Hybrid ARM.

examples: Borrower on ARM requests waiver due to administrative error → Servicer must deny; waiver prohibited.
Borrower on SBL Hybrid ARM is 7 days late → late charge cannot be waived even if this is first request.
Servicer issues waiver on ARM anyway → noncompliance; must reverse and collect late charge.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Servicing corrective action and potential reimbursement to Freddie Mac

examples: Servicer collects monthly payments and remits interest and principal per remittance rules
Servicer monitors insurance renewals and enforces coverage requirements
Servicer initiates default management after sustained delinquency

business_rules.rule_name: Third late charge waiver requires 12-month auto-debit
business_rules.description: A Servicer may only agree to a third late charge waiver request if the Borrower agrees to auto-debit the required payment for a minimum of the next 12 months (or, for a fixed-rate SBL Mortgage, is already required to auto-debit). The waiver must be approved by a supervisor (or higher manager). The Servicer must notify Freddie Mac within 30 days of the effective date by (1) creating a CRT record showing the satisfied delegation requirement and (2) delivering via DMS a completed General Servicing Request with supporting documentation. The Servicer must document the waiver and retain evidence in the Mortgage File.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when processing a Borrower’s third request to waive late charge collection
business_rules.consequences: If conditions met, Servicer may approve; if not, waiver not allowed without Freddie approval
business_rules.exceptions: Fixed-rate SBL Mortgage may already require auto-debit

source_reference.source_text: The Servicer may only agree to a third waiver request if the Borrower agrees to, or in the case of a fixed -rate SBL Mortgage, is already required to, auto -debit the required payment for a minimum of the next 12 months. The waiver must be approved by a supervisor or a higher -level manager of the Servicer and the Servicer must notify Freddie Mac of any actions taken under the delegated approval within 30 days of the effective date of the approval by taking the following actions: 1.Creating a record in CRT indicating the specific Servicer Delegation Requirement that has been satisfied. 2.Electronically delivering to Freddie Mac via DMS a completed General Servicing Request , along with any supporting documentation.

examples: Borrower requests 3rd waiver and signs up for auto-debit for next 12 months → Servicer may approve with supervisor approval and 30-day CRT/DMS notice.
Borrower requests 3rd waiver but refuses auto-debit → Servicer cannot approve under delegation; must seek Freddie Mac approval if pursuing waiver.
Servicer approves 3rd waiver but fails to notify Freddie Mac within 30 days → process violation even if waiver otherwise justified.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Servicing corrective action; delegated authority may be restricted

examples: Servicer collects monthly payments and remits interest and principal per remittance rules
Servicer monitors insurance renewals and enforces coverage requirements
Servicer initiates default management after sustained delinquency

dependencies.rationale: BR_002_006 describes conditions under which a third waiver request may be approved. BR_002_005 categorically prohibits any waiver for an ARM or SBL Hybrid ARM. Therefore, BR_002_005 supersedes/blocks BR_002_006 when the product type is ARM/SBL Hybrid ARM.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If override is not enforced, the servicer may unlawfully/impermissibly waive late charges on ARM/SBL Hybrid ARM, triggering Guide noncompliance, make-whole/remittance corrections, and potential servicing remedies.

business_rules.rule_name: Form 1140 Part I notice due within 14 days for $25k/$50k
business_rules.description: For a Mortgage secured by a Property that suffers loss or damage meeting specified conditions (including: SBL Mortgage with estimated loss > $25,000; non-SBL Mortgage with estimated loss > $50,000; event of default; or death/serious injury), the Servicer must, as soon as possible but no later than 14 calendar days after learning of the loss/damage (regardless of whether the Borrower will submit an insurance claim), complete Form 1140 Part I – Notification and upload it to DMS using “File Submission” → “Submit to Hazard Loss.”
business_rules.conditions: Applies when Servicer learns of casualty loss meeting listed thresholds/conditions
business_rules.consequences: If timely filed, hazard loss reporting is compliant; if late/missed, servicing noncompliance
business_rules.exceptions: None; applies regardless of insurance claim intent

source_reference.source_text: The Mortgage is an SBL Mortgage and the estimated loss is greater than $25,000 • The Mortgage is a non -SBL Mortgage and the estimated loss is greater than $50,000 As soon as possible, but in no event more than 14 calendar days after a Servicer learns that a loss or damage has occurred with respect to a Property, regardless of whether the Borrower plans to submit an insurance claim, the Servicer must : • Complete Form 1140 , Part I - Notification • Upload the completed form to the Document Management System (DMS), using the “File Submission” link to “Submit to Hazard Loss”

examples: SBL loan: storm damage estimated at $40,000; Servicer learns on 04/01 → Form 1140 Part I must be uploaded by 04/15.
Non-SBL loan: water damage estimated at $55,000; Servicer learns on 06/10 → must submit by 06/24 even if Borrower won’t file claim.
Loss estimated at $20,000 on SBL with no default/death/serious injury → Part I trigger may not be met under the dollar threshold.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Servicing corrective action; Freddie Mac may direct alternate action

examples: Servicer collects monthly payments and remits interest and principal per remittance rules
Servicer monitors insurance renewals and enforces coverage requirements
Servicer initiates default management after sustained delinquency

business_rules.rule_name: Restoration plan approval required at 10% units or $500k/10% UPB
business_rules.description: Freddie Mac approval of the Borrower’s restoration plans is required if one or more listed conditions exists, including: (1) damaged units are ≥ 10% of total units; (2) estimated loss > $500,000 or > 10% of outstanding UPB; (3) there has been an event of default; (4) death or serious injury; (5) for non-SBL, estimated loss exceeds Borrower Proof of Loss Maximum; (6) if not securitized and Risk Rating > 6 and estimated loss > $50,000; (7) insurance coverage insufficient and estimated loss > $50,000.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when assessing whether Freddie Mac approval is required for restoration plans
business_rules.consequences: If any trigger met, Freddie Mac approval required and Part II process applies; else Part II not required
business_rules.exceptions: If none of listed conditions exist, Servicer not required to complete Part II

source_reference.section_id: Section 43.8(a)(3) Form 1140 Part II – Restoration Plan for Approval (02/24/26 update)
source_reference.source_text: If one or more of the following conditions exists, Freddie Mac approval of the Borrower’s restoration plans is required: • There has been an event of default under the Loan Documents • Death or serious injury has resulted from the event • The percentage of units that sustained any damage is 10 percent or more of the Property’s total units • The estimated loss amount is greater than $500,000 or 10 percent of the outstanding UPB • For non -SBL Mortgages, the estimated loss exceeds the Borrower Proof of Loss Maximum (as defined in the Loan Agreement) • The Mortgage has not been securitized, has a Risk Rating greater than six and the estimated loss is greater than $50,000

examples: Property has 200 units; 25 units damaged (12.5%) → Freddie Mac approval required (≥10%).
Estimated loss $600,000 on any loan → approval required (> $500,000) even if few units damaged.
Not securitized, Risk Rating 7, estimated loss $60,000 → approval required (RR>6 and loss>$50k).
business_rules.enforcement_action: Freddie Mac approval requirement; disbursement restrictions may apply

examples: Servicer collects monthly payments and remits interest and principal per remittance rules
Servicer monitors insurance renewals and enforces coverage requirements
Servicer initiates default management after sustained delinquency

dependencies.rationale: BR_002_007 mandates a prompt notification and DMS upload when the servicer learns of loss/damage above thresholds. BR_002_008 then evaluates whether Freddie Mac approval is required based on loss magnitude, unit impact, defaults, and related triggers. Sequencing supports a consistent workflow: notify, then assess approval needs using the same event data.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_002_007 is delayed or omitted, downstream escalation/approval tracking under BR_002_008 may be delayed, causing restoration to proceed without required approvals or causing Freddie Mac to receive incomplete/late reporting.

business_rules.rule_name: Partial release title update must be dated within 45 days of Form 1125
business_rules.description: For partial releases requiring Freddie Mac approval, the Servicer must deliver a title update report dated not earlier than 45 days before the date of Form 1125 (Borrower Application for Partial Release or Easement).
business_rules.conditions: Applies when submitting partial release request package requiring Freddie Mac approval
business_rules.consequences: If title update is older than 45 days, submission is incomplete and may be rejected/delayed
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.section_id: Chapter 43 partial release request items (02/24/26), item 5
source_reference.source_text: The Servicer must deliver each of the following documents: 1. A completed Property Partial Release Request , which must include the Servicer's analysis along with the Servicer's recommendation for action 2. Form 1125 , Borrower Application for Partial Release or Easement, completed and signed by the Borrower and any Guarantor(s) ... 5. Title update report dated not earlier than 45 days before the date of Form 1125, Borrower Application for Partial Release or Easement

examples: Form 1125 dated 07/15 → title update must be dated 06/01 or later (within 45 days).
Form 1125 dated 11/30 → title update dated 09/30 is too old (>45 days) and must be refreshed.
Servicer submits a 60-day-old title update → Freddie Mac requests re-delivery before review can proceed.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Request for additional documentation; processing delay

examples: Servicer collects monthly payments and remits interest and principal per remittance rules
Servicer monitors insurance renewals and enforces coverage requirements
Servicer initiates default management after sustained delinquency

business_rules.rule_name: Partial release fee split 50/50 and no extra fees
business_rules.description: For partial releases requiring Freddie Mac approval, the Servicer must charge the Borrower a nonrefundable review/processing fee in the amount specified in the Loan Documents (or if not specified, Exhibit 10). The Servicer must remit 50% of the review/processing fee to Freddie Mac (if applicable) and may retain the remaining 50%. The Servicer may not charge the Borrower any additional fees for processing the application. At the time the request is delivered to Freddie Mac, the Servicer must remit Freddie Mac’s portion by wire transfer.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when submitting a partial release request that requires Freddie Mac approval
business_rules.consequences: If compliant, fee handling is accepted; if not, Freddie Mac may require reimbursement/corrective action
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.section_id: Chapter 43 partial release fees and remittance (02/24/26)
source_reference.source_text: For all partial releases that require Freddie Mac approval, the Servicer must charge the Borrower a nonrefundable review/processing fee for an application in the amount specified in the Loan Documents, or, if no amount is specified, in the amount set forth in Exhibit 10. The Servicer must remit to Freddie Mac 50 percent of the review/processing fee, if applicable, and may retain the remaining 50 percent. The Servicer may not charge the Borrower any additional fees for processing an application for approval of a partial release. At the time of delivery of the partial release request to Freddie Mac, the Servicer must remit to Freddie Mac by wire transfer the Freddie Mac portion of the review/processing fee.

examples: Loan docs specify $2,000 fee → Servicer collects $2,000, remits $1,000 to Freddie Mac by wire at delivery, retains $1,000; no extra admin fee allowed.
Loan docs silent; Exhibit 10 fee applies → same 50/50 remittance requirement and wire at delivery.
Servicer charges an extra $500 “legal review” fee → prohibited additional fee; must be reversed/refunded.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Fee reimbursement to Freddie Mac; servicing corrective action

examples: Servicer collects monthly payments and remits interest and principal per remittance rules
Servicer monitors insurance renewals and enforces coverage requirements
Servicer initiates default management after sustained delinquency

dependencies.rationale: BR_002_009 ensures the package includes a current title update report (≤45 days). BR_002_010 ensures correct fee charging/remittance and prohibits extra fees. They operate in parallel but together determine whether a partial release submission is complete and compliant.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If either fails, Freddie Mac may reject/delay the request: outdated title (risk of undisclosed liens) or incorrect fees (billing/remittance issues and corrective action).

business_rules.rule_name: ARM interest rate adjustment notice must be sent at least 15 calendar days before new payment due date
business_rules.description: For an ARM or an SBL Hybrid ARM, the Servicer must provide the Borrower advance written notice of each interest rate adjustment and each new monthly payment due under the Note. The notice must be delivered in the manner specified in the Loan Documents for giving notices, and must be given at least 15 calendar days prior to the due date of the new monthly payment.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to ARM and SBL Hybrid ARM Mortgages when payment amount changes
business_rules.consequences: If notice is timely, borrower notification requirement is satisfied; if late/missing, it is a servicing compliance breach requiring corrective action
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.section_id: 43.20 Advance written notice to the Borrower for ARM payments (06/30/16)
source_reference.source_text: 43.20 Advance written notice to the Borrower for ARM payments (06/30/16) The Servicer must provide the Borrower with advance written notice of each interest rate adjustment and each new monthly payment due under the Note for an ARM or an SBL Hybrid ARM. The Servicer must give each such notice in the manner specified in the Loan Documents for the giving of notices, and the Servicer must give each such notice at least 15 calendar days prior to the due date of the new monthly payment.

examples: New ARM payment is due 10/01; Servicer must send the written notice no later than 09/16 (15 calendar days prior).
Servicer sends notice by the contract-required delivery method (e.g., mailed to notice address) 20 days before due date; compliant.
Servicer emails notice 7 days before due date when Loan Documents require mailed notice and 15 days lead time; noncompliant and must be remediated.
business_rules.extraction_notes: Explicit numeric notice timing and scope (ARM and SBL Hybrid ARM).

examples: Servicer collects monthly payments and remits interest and principal per remittance rules
Servicer monitors insurance renewals and enforces coverage requirements
Servicer initiates default management after sustained delinquency

business_rules.rule_name: No payoff request 30 days pre-maturity triggers required contact attempts
business_rules.description: If the Servicer has not received a request for a payoff statement by the 30th day prior to the Mortgage maturity date, the Servicer must attempt to contact either (a) the Borrower by telephone to confirm the Borrower’s source of payoff funds, or (b) the new lender directly to confirm its commitment in a third-party refinance.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when no payoff statement request exists by day -30 before maturity date
business_rules.consequences: If not performed, Servicer fails Guide servicing responsibility and may face remedies
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.section_id: Guide Chapter 43 – Specific Servicing Responsibilities; payoff statement / maturity outreach
source_reference.source_text: If the Servicer has not received a request for a payoff statement by the 30th day prior to the maturity date of the Mortgage, the Servicer must attempt to contact: • The Borrower by telephone to confirm Borrower’s source of funds to pay off the Mortgage at maturity, or • The new lender directly for confirmation of its commitment to the Borrower, in the case of a third -party refinance.

examples: Maturity is August 31; on August 1 no payoff request exists—servicer calls borrower to confirm payoff funds source.
Borrower indicates refinance; servicer contacts the new lender to confirm commitment rather than relying solely on borrower statement.
Servicer does nothing until after maturity; Freddie Mac cites noncompliance and may impose servicing remedies.
business_rules.extraction_notes: Explicit 30th-day pre-maturity trigger and required outreach actions.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Servicing remedies under Purchase and Servicing Documents

examples: Servicer collects monthly payments and remits interest and principal per remittance rules
Servicer monitors insurance renewals and enforces coverage requirements
Servicer initiates default management after sustained delinquency

business_rules.rule_name: Rate reset notice window 180 to 110 days and servicer action in 10 days
business_rules.description: For Rate Reset Mortgages, the Borrower must notify the Servicer of intent to exercise the Rate Reset option no earlier than 180 days and no later than 110 days prior to the original term maturity date. Within 10 days after receiving the Borrower’s notice, the Servicer must notify Freddie Mac Multifamily Asset Management, Structured Transactions and deliver required items including an AIF dated within 60 days prior to submission and the Servicer’s written analysis of Borrower compliance with Loan Document requirements.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when Borrower elects Rate Reset option to extend term and reset rate
business_rules.consequences: If timelines/documents not met, Freddie Mac may delay/deny eligibility determination
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.section_id: Guide Chapter 43 – Specific Servicing Responsibilities; 43.15(a) Rate Reset Mortgages
source_reference.source_text: Within 10 days following receipt of notice that the Borrower wishes to exercise its option to extend the term of a Rate Reset Mortgage and reset the interest rate, the Servicer must notify Freddie Mac Multifamily Asset Management, Structured Transactions .  The Borrower must notify the Servicer of its intent to exercise the Rate Reset option no earlier than 180 days, and no later than 110 days prior to the maturity date of the original term.  The Servicer must deliver the following items to Freddie Mac Multi family Asset Management, Structured Transactions : ... 3. An AIF, dated within 60 days prior to submission 4. The Servicer's written analysis

examples: Maturity is Dec 31; borrower sends notice on July 15 (169 days prior)—notice is within 180–110 window; servicer must notify Freddie Mac by July 25 and include an AIF dated within 60 days before submission.
Borrower sends notice 95 days prior to maturity; it is later than 110 days and fails the timing requirement, so servicer escalates to Freddie Mac for direction and likely denial.
Servicer submits AIF dated 120 days before submission; package is noncompliant and must be updated to meet the 60-day currency requirement.
business_rules.extraction_notes: Multiple explicit deadlines (180/110 borrower window, 10-day servicer notice, 60-day AIF currency).
business_rules.enforcement_action: Denial/delay of rate reset approval; servicing findings

examples: Servicer collects monthly payments and remits interest and principal per remittance rules
Servicer monitors insurance renewals and enforces coverage requirements
Servicer initiates default management after sustained delinquency

business_rules.rule_name: Servicer cannot modify loan terms and REMIC actions require counsel opinion
business_rules.description: The Servicer must not modify, waive, or release any term of any Note, Security Instrument, or other Loan Document; accept any prepayment; or consent to postponement of Borrower performance except as authorized by the Purchase and Servicing Documents. For Mortgages included in a REMIC securitization, the Servicer must not knowingly or intentionally take (or fail to take) any action that adversely affects REMIC status or results in REMIC tax under IRC 860F(a) or 860G(d); unless waived by Freddie Mac, the Servicer must deliver an acceptable counsel opinion at no expense to Freddie Mac confirming no adverse REMIC impact and no REMIC tax.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to all Servicers; additional requirements when Mortgage is in a REMIC
business_rules.consequences: Unauthorized action is prohibited and may trigger servicing remedies and legal/tax exposure
business_rules.exceptions: Actions authorized by Purchase and Servicing Documents or waived by Freddie Mac

source_reference.section_id: Guide Chapter 43 – Specific Servicing Responsibilities; 43.14(a) Limits on Servicer
source_reference.source_text: The Servicer must not modify, waive or release any term of any Note, Security Instrument or other Loan Document, accept any prepayment, or consent to any postponement of performance by any Borrower of any obligation under a Note, Security Instrument or other Loan Document except as authorized by the Purchase and Servicing Documents. If a Mortgage is included in a REMIC Securitization, the Servicer will not knowingly or intentionally take any action ... that (i) adversely affects or impairs the status of the REMIC trust as a REMIC or (ii) results in the REMIC trust incur ring a tax under Section 860F (a) or Section 860G(d) ... unless waived by Freddie Mac, the Servicer must deliver an opinion of counsel

examples: Servicer agrees to waive a late fee without delegated authority; this is a prohibited waiver unless authorized by the Purchase and Servicing Documents.
Mortgage is in a REMIC and servicer proposes a modification; servicer must obtain (unless waived) counsel opinion confirming no REMIC status impairment and no 860F/860G tax.
Borrower requests postponed performance on a covenant; servicer cannot consent unless the servicing documents expressly authorize it or Freddie Mac grants approval.
business_rules.extraction_notes: Strong prohibition language; includes specific Internal Revenue Code sections and waiver concept; limited numeric content.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Servicing transfer, indemnification, or other remedies under servicing docs

examples: Servicer collects monthly payments and remits interest and principal per remittance rules
Servicer monitors insurance renewals and enforces coverage requirements
Servicer initiates default management after sustained delinquency

business_rules.rule_name: Deficient payment with reserves uses lower of $1,000 or escrow
business_rules.description: If a payment deficiency occurs on a Mortgage with Reserves and the Mortgage is not otherwise in default: (1) if the deficiency is less than or equal to the lower of $1,000 or the escrow payment due, the Servicer must reduce the amount credited to the Reserve balance by the deficiency, reflect DDLPI as current, and notify the Borrower. (2) if the deficiency is greater than $1,000 or the escrow payment due, the Servicer must credit the entire payment to the Reserve balance or a suspense account until full payment is received, keep DDLPI unchanged, inform the Borrower the deficiency is a Delinquency, and collect the deficiency before applying further payments (except to Reserve/suspense).
business_rules.conditions: Applies when Mortgage has Reserves and is not otherwise in default and payment is deficient.
business_rules.consequences: Proper handling preserves DDLPI accuracy; improper handling creates delinquency/reporting errors.
business_rules.exceptions: If Mortgage is in default, different rule applies (credit entire payment to Reserve/suspense).

source_reference.section_id: Chapter 53 – Application of payments: differences in collection (10/31/12) – Deficient payment with Reserves
source_reference.source_text: a. Deficient payment on any Mortgage with Reserves (10/31/12) 1. If the deficiency is less than or equal to the lower of $1,000 or the amount of the Escrow payment due, the Servicer must: • Reduce the amount credited to the Reserve balance by the deficiency • Reflect the Due Date of the Last Paid Installment (DDLPI) as current • Notify the Borrower of the deficiency 2. If the deficiency is greater than $1,000 or the amount of the Reserve payment due, the Servicer must: • Credit the entire payment to the Reserve balance or a suspense account until the Servicer receives the full payment • Ensure that the DDLPI remains the same

examples: Escrow due is $750 and deficiency is $600: since $600 ≤ lower of $1,000 and $750 (= $750), reduce reserve credit by $600 and keep DDLPI current.
Escrow due is $750 and deficiency is $800: since $800 > $750, credit entire payment to reserve/suspense and do not advance DDLPI until full payment is received.
Escrow due is $1,500 and deficiency is $900: since $900 ≤ lower of $1,000 and $1,500 (= $1,000), treat as small deficiency and keep DDLPI current.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Servicer corrective action plan; remittance/reporting exception

examples: Servicer collects monthly payments and remits interest and principal per remittance rules
Servicer monitors insurance renewals and enforces coverage requirements
Servicer initiates default management after sustained delinquency

business_rules.rule_name: Deficient payment without reserves uses $100 threshold and suspense
business_rules.description: If a payment deficiency occurs on an amortizing Mortgage without Reserves and the Mortgage is not otherwise in default: (1) if deficiency is $100 or less, reduce the amount credited to principal by the deficiency, reflect DDLPI as current, notify the Borrower, and collect the deficiency before applying any further payment except to a suspense account; (2) if deficiency is greater than $100, credit the entire payment to a suspense account until full payment is received, keep DDLPI unchanged, inform the Borrower the deficiency constitutes a Delinquency, and collect the deficiency before applying further payments except to a suspense account.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to amortizing Mortgages without Reserves when payment received is deficient.
business_rules.consequences: Compliant processing sets correct DDLPI/delinquency; noncompliance misstates delinquency and cash.
business_rules.exceptions: Separate rules apply for interest-only without reserves and for Mortgages in default.

source_reference.section_id: Chapter 53 – Deficient payment on an amortizing Mortgage without Reserves (10/31/12)
source_reference.source_text: b. Deficient payment on an amortizing Mortgage without Reserves (10/31/12) 1. If the deficiency is $100 or less, the Servicer must: • Reduce the amount credited to the principal balance by the deficiency • Reflect the DDLPI as current • Notify the Borrower of the deficiency ... 2. If the deficiency is greater than $100, the Servicer must: • Credit the entire payment to a suspense account until the Servicer receives the full payment • Ensure that the DDLPI remains the same as before the deficient payment was recorded • Inform the Borrower that the deficiency constitutes a Delinquency

examples: Payment is short by $75 on amortizing loan with no reserves: reduce principal credit by $75 and keep DDLPI current; collect $75 before applying further payments (except to suspense).
Payment is short by $250: place entire payment in suspense, DDLPI does not advance, and borrower is told it is a delinquency.
Borrower makes two consecutive deficient payments: delinquency rules apply and funds must be credited to suspense per Guide.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Servicer corrective action plan; remittance/reporting exception

examples: Servicer collects monthly payments and remits interest and principal per remittance rules
Servicer monitors insurance renewals and enforces coverage requirements
Servicer initiates default management after sustained delinquency

business_rules.rule_name: Loan term over 10 years requires replacement reserve review at 10th anniversary
business_rules.description: For a Mortgage with a loan term longer than 10 years, if the Loan Documents allow adjustment of the Replacement Reserve amount following an updated property condition report, then on the 10th anniversary of the Mortgage Origination Date the Servicer must conduct a re-evaluation of the adequacy of the Replacement Reserve.
business_rules.conditions: When Mortgage term > 10 years and Loan Documents authorize reserve adjustment after updated PCR
business_rules.consequences: If met: reserve adequacy is reassessed per Guide; if violated: servicing noncompliance and corrective action
business_rules.exceptions: Only applies if Loan Documents provide for reserve adjustment following updated PCR

source_reference.section_id: Chapter 39 – 39.6(f)(1) Mortgages with terms longer than 10 years (02/24/26)
source_reference.source_text: 1. For a Mortgage with a loan term longer than 10 years, the Replacement Reserve requirements in the Loan Documents may provide that the lender may adjust the Replacement Reserve amount following receipt of an updated property condition report. If the Loan Documents provide for such an adjustment, then on the 10th anniversary of the Origination Date of the Mortgage, the Servicer must conduct a re -evaluation of the adequacy of the Replacement Reserve.

examples: 12-year term loan with reserve-adjustment clause: on the 10-year origination anniversary, Servicer performs required replacement reserve adequacy review.
15-year term loan without any clause allowing reserve adjustment after updated PCR: the specific 10th anniversary re-evaluation requirement does not apply under this paragraph.
11-year term loan with adjustment clause: Servicer fails to perform review at 10th anniversary → noncompliant servicing finding.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Servicer corrective action plan and potential indemnification

examples: Servicer collects monthly payments and remits interest and principal per remittance rules
Servicer monitors insurance renewals and enforces coverage requirements
Servicer initiates default management after sustained delinquency

business_rules.rule_name: Replacement reserve adjustment recommendation due within 15 days
business_rules.description: After the Servicer receives the updated property condition report or conducts the required property inspection (as applicable for loans with terms longer than 10 years where adjustment is permitted), the Servicer must electronically submit a recommendation to Freddie Mac on the General Servicing Request within 15 days regarding whether the Replacement Reserve amount should be adjusted.
